question_html: Solve the system of linear equation, using matrix method x - y + 2z = 7; 3x + 4y - 5z = -5; 2x - y + 3z = 12
question_text: Solve the system of linear equation, using matrix method x - y + 2z = 7; 3x + 4y - 5z = -5; 2x - y + 3z = 12
explanation: Matrix form of given equations is AX = B {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 3&4&{ - 5} \\ 2&{ - 1}&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7 \\ { - 5} \\ {12} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Here {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 3&4&{ - 5} \\ 2&{ - 1}&3 \end{array}} \right],X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7 \\ { - 5} \\ {12} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left| A \right| = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 3&4&{ - 5} \\ 2&{ - 1}&3 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} = 1(12 - 5) - (-1)(9 + 10) + 2(-3 - 8) = 7 + 19 - 22 = 4 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 A 11 = 7, A 12 = -19, A 13 = -11 A 21 =1, A 22 = -1, A 23 = -1 A 31 = -3, A 32 =11, A 33 = 7 adj A= {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&1&{ - 3} \\ { - 19}&{ - 1}&{11} \\ { - 11}&{ - 1}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Therefore, solution is unique and {tex}X = {A^{ - 1}}B = \frac{1}{{\left| A \right|}}\left( {adj.A} \right)B{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \frac{1}{4}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&1&{ - 3} \\ { - 19}&{ - 1}&{11} \\ { - 11}&{ - 1}&7 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7 \\ { - 5} \\ {12} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{4}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {49 - 5 - 36} \\ { - 133 + 5 + 132} \\ { - 77 + 5 + 84} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{4}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8 \\ 4 \\ {12} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Therefore, x = 2, y = 1 and z = 3
solution: Matrix form of given equations is AX = B {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 3&4&{ - 5} \\ 2&{ - 1}&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7 \\ { - 5} \\ {12} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Here {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 3&4&{ - 5} \\ 2&{ - 1}&3 \end{array}} \right],X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7 \\ { - 5} \\ {12} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left| A \right| = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 3&4&{ - 5} \\ 2&{ - 1}&3 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} = 1(12 - 5) - (-1)(9 + 10) + 2(-3 - 8) = 7 + 19 - 22 = 4 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 A 11 = 7, A 12 = -19, A 13 = -11 A 21 =1, A 22 = -1, A 23 = -1 A 31 = -3, A 32 =11, A 33 = 7 adj A= {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&1&{ - 3} \\ { - 19}&{ - 1}&{11} \\ { - 11}&{ - 1}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Therefore, solution is unique and {tex}X = {A^{ - 1}}B = \frac{1}{{\left| A \right|}}\left( {adj.A} \right)B{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \frac{1}{4}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&1&{ - 3} \\ { - 19}&{ - 1}&{11} \\ { - 11}&{ - 1}&7 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7 \\ { - 5} \\ {12} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{4}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {49 - 5 - 36} \\ { - 133 + 5 + 132} \\ { - 77 + 5 + 84} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{4}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8 \\ 4 \\ {12} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Therefore, x = 2, y = 1 and z = 3
solution_html:
Matrix form of given equations is AX = B

{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 3&4&{ - 5} \\ 2&{ - 1}&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7 \\ { - 5} \\ {12} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

Here {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 3&4&{ - 5} \\ 2&{ - 1}&3 \end{array}} \right],X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7 \\ { - 5} \\ {12} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \left| A \right| = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 3&4&{ - 5} \\ 2&{ - 1}&3 \end{array}} \right|{/tex}

= 1(12 - 5) - (-1)(9 + 10) + 2(-3 - 8)

= 7 + 19 - 22 = 4 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0

A11 = 7, A12 = -19, A13 = -11
A21 =1, A22 = -1, A23 = -1
A31 = -3, A32 =11, A33 = 7

adj A={tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&1&{ - 3} \\ { - 19}&{ - 1}&{11} \\ { - 11}&{ - 1}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

Therefore, solution is unique and {tex}X = {A^{ - 1}}B = \frac{1}{{\left| A \right|}}\left( {adj.A} \right)B{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \frac{1}{4}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&1&{ - 3} \\ { - 19}&{ - 1}&{11} \\ { - 11}&{ - 1}&7 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7 \\ { - 5} \\ {12} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{1}{4}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {49 - 5 - 36} \\ { - 133 + 5 + 132} \\ { - 77 + 5 + 84} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{1}{4}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8 \\ 4 \\ {12} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

Therefore, x = 2, y = 1 and z = 3

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}be such that A-1 = kA, then find the value of k.
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} be such that A -1 = kA, then find the value of k.
explanation: According to question, A -1 = kA {tex}\frac{1}{A}=k A{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{1}{A^{2}}=\frac{1}{\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]}=\frac{1}{19\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]}{/tex} Also. {tex}|k|=\frac{1}{|A|^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\left|A^{2}\right|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{| 19\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{19\times1}=\frac{1}{19}{/tex} Hence, k = {tex}\frac{1}{19}{/tex}
solution: According to question, A -1 = kA {tex}\frac{1}{A}=k A{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{1}{A^{2}}=\frac{1}{\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]}=\frac{1}{19\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]}{/tex} Also. {tex}|k|=\frac{1}{|A|^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\left|A^{2}\right|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{| 19\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{19\times1}=\frac{1}{19}{/tex} Hence, k = {tex}\frac{1}{19}{/tex}
solution_html:
According to question, 
A-1 = kA
{tex}\frac{1}{A}=k A{/tex}
{tex}k=\frac{1}{A^{2}}=\frac{1}{\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]}=\frac{1}{19\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]}{/tex}
Also. {tex}|k|=\frac{1}{|A|^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\left|A^{2}\right|}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{| 19\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{19\times1}=\frac{1}{19}{/tex}
Hence, k = {tex}\frac{1}{19}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Given  {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {5} & {0} & {4} \\ {2} & {3} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right], B^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {3} & {3} \\ {1} & {4} & {3} \\ {1} & {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}. Compute (AB)-1
question_text: Given {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {5} & {0} & {4} \\ {2} & {3} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right], B^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {3} & {3} \\ {1} & {4} & {3} \\ {1} & {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} . Compute (AB) -1
solution_html:
{tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {5} & {0} & {4} \\ {2} & {3} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{a n d \ B^{-1}} =\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {3} & {3} \\ {1} & {4} & {3} \\ {1} & {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Here , (AB) – 1 = B–1 A–1
|A| = 5(3-4) + 4(4-3)
      =5 (-1) +4 (1) = -1
Cofactors of A are:
C11 = – 1 C21 = 8 C31 = – 12
C12 = 0 C22 = 1 C32 = – 2
C13 = 1 C23 = – 10 C33 = 15
adj A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {\mathrm{C}_{11}} & {\mathrm{C}_{12}} & {\mathrm{C}_{13}} \\ {\mathrm{C}_{21}} & {\mathrm{C}_{22}} & {\mathrm{C}_{23}} \\ {\mathrm{C}_{31}} & {\mathrm{C}_{32}} & {\mathrm{C}_{33}} \end{array}\right]^{\mathrm{T}}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {0} & {1} \\ {8} & {1} & {-10} \\ {-12} & {-2} & {15} \end{array}\right]^{\mathrm{T}}{/tex}
So, adj A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {8} & {-12} \\ {0} & {1} & {-2} \\ {1} & {-10} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, A – 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{-1}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-1} & {8} & {-12} \\ {0} & {1} & {-2} \\ {1} & {-10} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {1} & {-8} & {12} \\ {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {10} & {-15} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
(AB) – 1 = B–1 A–1
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {3} & {3} \\ {1} & {4} & {3} \\ {1} & {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {-8} & {12} \\ {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {10} & {-15} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

Hence,(AB)-1 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-2} & {19} & {-27} \\ {-2} & {18} & {-25} \\ {-3} & {29} & {42} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

question_text: If A = 3 B, where A and B are square matrices of order 3 and | B | = 2, then find | A |.

solution: We have, {tex}|A|{/tex} = {tex}|3B|{/tex} = 3 3 {tex}\times|B|{/tex} = 27 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 54

question_html: Given A = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 3} \\ { - 4}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, compute A-1 and show that 2A-1 = 9I - A.
question_text: Given A = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 3} \\ { - 4}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , compute A -1 and show that 2A -1 = 9I - A.
explanation: According to the question, A = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 3} \\ { - 4}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Here, {tex}\left| A \right| = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 3} \\ { - 4}&7 \end{array}} \right| = {/tex} 14 - 12 = 2 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 {tex} {{\text{If A = }}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right],{\text{then}}\,{\text{adj(A)}} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} d&{ - b} \\ { - c}&a \end{array}} \right]}{/tex} Now, adj(A) = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&3 \\ 4&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore {{\text{A}}^{ - 1}} = \frac{1}{{|{\text{A}}|}}{\text{adj(A)}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&3 \\ 4&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...........(i) Now, consider RHS {tex} = 9I - A{/tex} {tex} = 9\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 3} \\ { - 4}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&0 \\ 0&9 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 3} \\ { - 4}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&3 \\ 4&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} = 2 A -1 (Using Eq.(i)) {tex}= LHS{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution: According to the question, A = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 3} \\ { - 4}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Here, {tex}\left| A \right| = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 3} \\ { - 4}&7 \end{array}} \right| = {/tex} 14 - 12 = 2 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 {tex} {{\text{If A = }}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right],{\text{then}}\,{\text{adj(A)}} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} d&{ - b} \\ { - c}&a \end{array}} \right]}{/tex} Now, adj(A) = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&3 \\ 4&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore {{\text{A}}^{ - 1}} = \frac{1}{{|{\text{A}}|}}{\text{adj(A)}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&3 \\ 4&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...........(i) Now, consider RHS {tex} = 9I - A{/tex} {tex} = 9\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 3} \\ { - 4}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&0 \\ 0&9 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 3} \\ { - 4}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&3 \\ 4&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} = 2 A -1 (Using Eq.(i)) {tex}= LHS{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution_html:
According to the question, A = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 3} \\ { - 4}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Here, {tex}\left| A \right| = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 3} \\ { - 4}&7 \end{array}} \right| = {/tex}14 - 12 = 2 {tex}\ne{/tex}0
{tex} {{\text{If A = }}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right],{\text{then}}\,{\text{adj(A)}} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} d&{ - b} \\ { - c}&a \end{array}} \right]}{/tex}
Now, adj(A) = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&3 \\ 4&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {{\text{A}}^{ - 1}} = \frac{1}{{|{\text{A}}|}}{\text{adj(A)}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&3 \\ 4&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}...........(i)
Now, consider RHS
{tex} = 9I - A{/tex}
{tex} = 9\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 3} \\ { - 4}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&0 \\ 0&9 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 3} \\ { - 4}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
={tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&3 \\ 4&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
= 2 A-1 (Using Eq.(i))
{tex}= LHS{/tex}
Hence Proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A school wants to award its students for the values of Honesty, Regularity and Hardwork with a total cash award of ₹ 6000. Three times the award money for Hardwork added to that given for honesty amounts to ₹ 11000. The award money given for Honesty and Hard work together is double the one given for Regularity. Represent the above situation algebraically and find the award for each value, using matrix method. Apart from these values, namely, Honesty, Regularity and Hardwork, suggest one more value which the school must include for awards.
question_text: A school wants to award its students for the values of Honesty, Regularity and Hardwork with a total cash award of ₹ 6000. Three times the award money for Hardwork added to that given for honesty amounts to ₹ 11000. The award money given for Honesty and Hard work together is double the one given for Regularity. Represent the above situation algebraically and find the award for each value, using matrix method. Apart from these values, namely, Honesty, Regularity and Hardwork, suggest one more value which the school must include for awards.
explanation: Let the numbers are x, y, z be the cash awards for Honesty, Regularity and Hard Work x + y + z = 6000 …(i) Also, x + 3z = 11000 …(ii) Again, x – 2y + z = 0 …(iii) {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 6000 \\ 11000 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A X = B |A| = 1(0 + 6) – 1(1 – 3) + 1(– 2 – 0) = 1(6) – 1(– 2) – 2 = 6 + 2 – 2 = 6 Hence, the unique solution given by x = A – 1 B C 11 = (– 1) 1 + 1 (0 + 6) = 6 C 12 = (– 1) 1 + 2 (1 – 3) = C 13 = (– 1) 1 + 3 (– 2 – 0) = – 2 C 21 = (– 1) 2 + 1 (1 + 2) = – 3 C 22 = (– 1) 2 + 2 (1 – 1) = 0 C 23 = (– 1) 2 + 3 (– 2 – 1 ) = 3 C 31 = (– 1) 3 + 1 (3 – 0) = 3 C 32 = (– 1) 3 + 2 (3 – 1) = – 2 C 33 = (– 1) 3 + 3 (0 – 1) = – 1 {tex}\text { Adj } A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & 2 & -2 \\ -3 & 0 & 3 \\ 3 & -2 & -1 \end{array}\right]^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & -3 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & -2 \\ -2 & 3 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}X=A^{-1} B=\frac{1}{|A|}(\text { adj } A) B{/tex} {tex}X=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & -3 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & -2 \\ -2 & 3 & -1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 6000 \\ 11000 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}X=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{c} 36000-33000+0 \\ 12000+0-0 \\ -12000+33000-0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 3000 \\ 12000 \\ 21000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Hence, x = 500, y = 2000 and z = 3500
solution: Let the numbers are x, y, z be the cash awards for Honesty, Regularity and Hard Work x + y + z = 6000 …(i) Also, x + 3z = 11000 …(ii) Again, x – 2y + z = 0 …(iii) {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 6000 \\ 11000 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A X = B |A| = 1(0 + 6) – 1(1 – 3) + 1(– 2 – 0) = 1(6) – 1(– 2) – 2 = 6 + 2 – 2 = 6 Hence, the unique solution given by x = A – 1 B C 11 = (– 1) 1 + 1 (0 + 6) = 6 C 12 = (– 1) 1 + 2 (1 – 3) = C 13 = (– 1) 1 + 3 (– 2 – 0) = – 2 C 21 = (– 1) 2 + 1 (1 + 2) = – 3 C 22 = (– 1) 2 + 2 (1 – 1) = 0 C 23 = (– 1) 2 + 3 (– 2 – 1 ) = 3 C 31 = (– 1) 3 + 1 (3 – 0) = 3 C 32 = (– 1) 3 + 2 (3 – 1) = – 2 C 33 = (– 1) 3 + 3 (0 – 1) = – 1 {tex}\text { Adj } A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & 2 & -2 \\ -3 & 0 & 3 \\ 3 & -2 & -1 \end{array}\right]^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & -3 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & -2 \\ -2 & 3 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}X=A^{-1} B=\frac{1}{|A|}(\text { adj } A) B{/tex} {tex}X=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & -3 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & -2 \\ -2 & 3 & -1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 6000 \\ 11000 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}X=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{c} 36000-33000+0 \\ 12000+0-0 \\ -12000+33000-0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 3000 \\ 12000 \\ 21000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Hence, x = 500, y = 2000 and z = 3500
solution_html:
Let the numbers are x, y, z be the cash awards for Honesty, Regularity and Hard Work
x + y + z = 6000 …(i)
Also,
x + 3z = 11000 …(ii)
Again,
x – 2y + z = 0 …(iii)
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 6000 \\ 11000 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
A X = B
|A| = 1(0 + 6) – 1(1 – 3) + 1(– 2 – 0)
= 1(6) – 1(– 2) – 2
= 6 + 2 – 2
= 6
Hence, the unique solution given by x = A – 1B
C11 = (– 1)1 + 1 (0 + 6) = 6
C12 = (– 1)1 + 2 (1 – 3) = 
C13 = (– 1)1 + 3 (– 2 – 0) = – 2
C21 = (– 1)2 + 1 (1 + 2) = – 3
C22 = (– 1)2 + 2 (1 – 1) = 0
C23 = (– 1)2 + 3 (– 2 – 1 ) = 3
C31 = (– 1)3 + 1 (3 – 0) = 3
C32 = (– 1)3 + 2 (3 – 1) = – 2
C33 = (– 1)3 + 3 (0 – 1) = – 1
{tex}\text { Adj } A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & 2 & -2 \\ -3 & 0 & 3 \\ 3 & -2 & -1 \end{array}\right]^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & -3 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & -2 \\ -2 & 3 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}X=A^{-1} B=\frac{1}{|A|}(\text { adj } A) B{/tex}
{tex}X=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & -3 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & -2 \\ -2 & 3 & -1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 6000 \\ 11000 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}X=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{c} 36000-33000+0 \\ 12000+0-0 \\ -12000+33000-0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 3000 \\ 12000 \\ 21000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Hence, x = 500, y = 2000 and z = 3500

question_html: If for any {tex}2 \times 2{/tex} square matrix A, A(adj A)= {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 8 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}, then write the value of |A|.
question_text: If for any {tex}2 \times 2{/tex} square matrix A, A(adj A)= {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 8 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} , then write the value of |A|.
explanation: If for any {tex}2 \times 2{/tex} square matrix A, A(adj A)= {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 8 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} , then, we have to find out the value of |A|. Now, A(adjA) = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 8 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A(adjA)| = {tex}\left| \begin{array} { l l } { 8 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A||adj (A)|= 64-0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A| |A| 2-1 = 64 [ {tex}\because{/tex} |adj A|= |A| n-1 ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A| 2 = 64 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A| = {tex}\pm{/tex} 8
solution: If for any {tex}2 \times 2{/tex} square matrix A, A(adj A)= {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 8 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} , then, we have to find out the value of |A|. Now, A(adjA) = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 8 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A(adjA)| = {tex}\left| \begin{array} { l l } { 8 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A||adj (A)|= 64-0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A| |A| 2-1 = 64 [ {tex}\because{/tex} |adj A|= |A| n-1 ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A| 2 = 64 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A| = {tex}\pm{/tex} 8
solution_html:
If for any {tex}2 \times 2{/tex} square matrix A, A(adj A)= {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 8 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}, then, we  have to find out the value of |A|.

Now, A(adjA) = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 8 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}|A(adjA)| = {tex}\left| \begin{array} { l l } { 8 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A||adj (A)|= 64-0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A| |A|2-1 = 64  [{tex}\because{/tex}|adj A|= |A|n-1]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A|2 = 64 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A| = {tex}\pm{/tex}8

question_html:
If A {tex} = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 2 } & { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 4 } & { - 6 } & { 5 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}, find A-1. Using A-1 solve the system of equations 
{tex} \frac { 2 } { x } + \frac { 3 } { y } + \frac { 10 } { z } = 2 ; \frac { 4 } { x } - \frac { 6 } { y } + \frac { 5 } { z } = 5{/tex}; and {tex} \frac { 6 } { x } + \frac { 9 } { y } - \frac { 20 } { z } = - 4{/tex}.
question_text: If A {tex} = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 2 } & { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 4 } & { - 6 } & { 5 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} , find A -1 . Using A -1 solve the system of equations {tex} \frac { 2 } { x } + \frac { 3 } { y } + \frac { 10 } { z } = 2 ; \frac { 4 } { x } - \frac { 6 } { y } + \frac { 5 } { z } = 5{/tex} ; and {tex} \frac { 6 } { x } + \frac { 9 } { y } - \frac { 20 } { z } = - 4{/tex} .
explanation: If A {tex} = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 2 } & { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 4 } & { - 6 } & { 5 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} then, we have to find A -1 . Using A -1 , we have to solve the following system of equations, {tex} \frac { 2 } { x } + \frac { 3 } { y } + \frac { 10 } { z } = 2 ; \frac { 4 } { x } - \frac { 6 } { y } + \frac { 5 } { z } = 5{/tex} ; and {tex} \frac { 6 } { x } + \frac { 9 } { y } - \frac { 20 } { z } = - 4{/tex} . Suppose {tex} \frac { 1 } { x } = p , \frac { 1 } { y } = q{/tex} and {tex} \frac { 1 } { z } = r{/tex} Hence, the given equations become 2p + 3q + 10r =2 4p - 6q + 5r =5 6p + 9q - 20r = - 4 The system of above equations can be written as AX = B, where A {tex} = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 2 } & { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 4 } & { - 6 } & { 5 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} X = {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { l } { p } \\ { q } \\ { r } \end{array} \right] ,B = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 2 } \\ { 5 } \\ { - 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Here, |A| = {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 2 } & { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 4 } & { - 6 } & { 5 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} = 2(120 - 45) - 3(-80 - 30) + 10(36 + 36) =150+ 330+ 720=1200 {tex} \neq{/tex} 0 Thus, A is non-singular and thus its inverse exists. Therefore, the above system has a unique solution given by X = A -1 B Now,Cofactors of A are, A 11 = 75, A 21 = 150, A 31 = 75 A 12 = 110, A 22 = -100, A 32 = 30 A 13 = 72, A 23 = 0, A 33 = - 24 Therefore,adj (A) = {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 75 } & { 110 } & { 72 } \\ { 150 } & { - 100 } & { 0 } \\ { 75 } & { 30 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right]' = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 75 } & { 150 } & { 75 } \\ { 110 } & { - 100 } & { 30 } \\ { 72 } & { 0 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}A ^ { - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { | A | } ( \operatorname { adj } A ) = \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 75 } & { 150 } & { 75 } \\ { 110 } & { - 100 } & { 30 } \\ { 72 } & { 0 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Therefore, X = A -1 B {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { l } { p } \\ { q } \\ { r } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 75 } & { 150 } & { 75 } \\ { 110 } & { - 100 } & { 30 } \\ { 72 } & { 0 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 2 } \\ { 5 } \\ { - 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 150 + 750 - 300 } \\ { 220 - 500 - 120 } \\ { 144 + 0 + 96 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 900 - 300 } \\ { 220 - 620 } \\ { 144 + 96 } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 600 } \\ { - 400 } \\ { 240 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 1 / 2 } \\ { - 1 / 3 } \\ { 1 / 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad p = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , q = - \frac { 1 } { 3 } , r = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 2, y = - 3 and z = 5
solution: If A {tex} = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 2 } & { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 4 } & { - 6 } & { 5 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} then, we have to find A -1 . Using A -1 , we have to solve the following system of equations, {tex} \frac { 2 } { x } + \frac { 3 } { y } + \frac { 10 } { z } = 2 ; \frac { 4 } { x } - \frac { 6 } { y } + \frac { 5 } { z } = 5{/tex} ; and {tex} \frac { 6 } { x } + \frac { 9 } { y } - \frac { 20 } { z } = - 4{/tex} . Suppose {tex} \frac { 1 } { x } = p , \frac { 1 } { y } = q{/tex} and {tex} \frac { 1 } { z } = r{/tex} Hence, the given equations become 2p + 3q + 10r =2 4p - 6q + 5r =5 6p + 9q - 20r = - 4 The system of above equations can be written as AX = B, where A {tex} = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 2 } & { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 4 } & { - 6 } & { 5 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} X = {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { l } { p } \\ { q } \\ { r } \end{array} \right] ,B = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 2 } \\ { 5 } \\ { - 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Here, |A| = {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 2 } & { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 4 } & { - 6 } & { 5 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} = 2(120 - 45) - 3(-80 - 30) + 10(36 + 36) =150+ 330+ 720=1200 {tex} \neq{/tex} 0 Thus, A is non-singular and thus its inverse exists. Therefore, the above system has a unique solution given by X = A -1 B Now,Cofactors of A are, A 11 = 75, A 21 = 150, A 31 = 75 A 12 = 110, A 22 = -100, A 32 = 30 A 13 = 72, A 23 = 0, A 33 = - 24 Therefore,adj (A) = {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 75 } & { 110 } & { 72 } \\ { 150 } & { - 100 } & { 0 } \\ { 75 } & { 30 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right]' = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 75 } & { 150 } & { 75 } \\ { 110 } & { - 100 } & { 30 } \\ { 72 } & { 0 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}A ^ { - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { | A | } ( \operatorname { adj } A ) = \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 75 } & { 150 } & { 75 } \\ { 110 } & { - 100 } & { 30 } \\ { 72 } & { 0 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Therefore, X = A -1 B {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { l } { p } \\ { q } \\ { r } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 75 } & { 150 } & { 75 } \\ { 110 } & { - 100 } & { 30 } \\ { 72 } & { 0 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 2 } \\ { 5 } \\ { - 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 150 + 750 - 300 } \\ { 220 - 500 - 120 } \\ { 144 + 0 + 96 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 900 - 300 } \\ { 220 - 620 } \\ { 144 + 96 } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 600 } \\ { - 400 } \\ { 240 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 1 / 2 } \\ { - 1 / 3 } \\ { 1 / 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad p = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , q = - \frac { 1 } { 3 } , r = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 2, y = - 3 and z = 5
solution_html:
If A {tex} = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 2 } & { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 4 } & { - 6 } & { 5 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}then, we have to find A-1. Using A-1, we have to solve the following   system of equations,
{tex} \frac { 2 } { x } + \frac { 3 } { y } + \frac { 10 } { z } = 2 ; \frac { 4 } { x } - \frac { 6 } { y } + \frac { 5 } { z } = 5{/tex}; and {tex} \frac { 6 } { x } + \frac { 9 } { y } - \frac { 20 } { z } = - 4{/tex} .

Suppose {tex} \frac { 1 } { x } = p , \frac { 1 } { y } = q{/tex} and {tex} \frac { 1 } { z } = r{/tex}
Hence, the given equations become
2p + 3q + 10r =2
4p - 6q + 5r =5
6p + 9q - 20r = - 4
The system  of above equations can be written as AX = B, where
A{tex} = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 2 } & { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 4 } & { - 6 } & { 5 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
X  = {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { l } { p } \\ { q } \\ { r } \end{array} \right] ,B = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 2 } \\ { 5 } \\ { - 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Here, |A| = {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 2 } & { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 4 } & { - 6 } & { 5 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
= 2(120 - 45) - 3(-80 - 30) + 10(36 + 36)
=150+ 330+ 720=1200 {tex} \neq{/tex}0
Thus, A is non-singular and thus its inverse exists.
Therefore, the above system has a unique solution given by
X = A-1B
Now,Cofactors of A are,
A11 = 75, A21 = 150, A31 = 75
A12 = 110, A22 = -100, A32 = 30
A13 = 72, A23 = 0, A33 = - 24
Therefore,adj (A) = {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 75 } & { 110 } & { 72 } \\ { 150 } & { - 100 } & { 0 } \\ { 75 } & { 30 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right]' = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 75 } & { 150 } & { 75 } \\ { 110 } & { - 100 } & { 30 } \\ { 72 } & { 0 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}A ^ { - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { | A | } ( \operatorname { adj } A ) = \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 75 } & { 150 } & { 75 } \\ { 110 } & { - 100 } & { 30 } \\ { 72 } & { 0 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}Therefore, X = A-1 B
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { l } { p } \\ { q } \\ { r } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 75 } & { 150 } & { 75 } \\ { 110 } & { - 100 } & { 30 } \\ { 72 } & { 0 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 2 } \\ { 5 } \\ { - 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 150 + 750 - 300 } \\ { 220 - 500 - 120 } \\ { 144 + 0 + 96 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 900 - 300 } \\ { 220 - 620 } \\ { 144 + 96 } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 600 } \\ { - 400 } \\ { 240 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 1 / 2 } \\ { - 1 / 3 } \\ { 1 / 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad p = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , q = - \frac { 1 } { 3 } , r = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 2, y = - 3 and z = 5

question_html: If A is a 3 {tex}\times{/tex}3 matrix, then what will be the value of k if Det (A-1) = (Det A)k?
question_text: If A is a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix, then what will be the value of k if Det (A -1 ) = (Det A) k ?
explanation: we know that A -1 = {tex}\frac{ad j A}{|A|}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} |A -1 | = {tex}\frac{|A d j A|}{|A|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{|A|^{3-1}}{|A|}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} If A is a non singular matrix of order n, |adj (A)| = |A| n-1 ] = {tex}\frac{|A|^{2}}{|A|}{/tex} |A ​​​​​​-1 ​​​​|= |A| As given that |A -1 | = |A| k {tex}\therefore{/tex} k = 1
solution: we know that A -1 = {tex}\frac{ad j A}{|A|}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} |A -1 | = {tex}\frac{|A d j A|}{|A|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{|A|^{3-1}}{|A|}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} If A is a non singular matrix of order n, |adj (A)| = |A| n-1 ] = {tex}\frac{|A|^{2}}{|A|}{/tex} |A ​​​​​​-1 ​​​​|= |A| As given that |A -1 | = |A| k {tex}\therefore{/tex} k = 1
solution_html:
we know that
A-1 = {tex}\frac{ad j A}{|A|}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} |A-1| = {tex}\frac{|A d j A|}{|A|}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{|A|^{3-1}}{|A|}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} If A is a non singular matrix of order n, |adj (A)| = |A|n-1]
= {tex}\frac{|A|^{2}}{|A|}{/tex} 
|A​​​​​​-1​​​​|= |A|
As  given that |A-1| = |A|k 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k = 1

explanation: {tex}\left|A^{-1}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\left|A^{-1}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

= {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

question_html: If {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta } & { - x } & { 1 } \\ { \cos \theta } & { 1 } & { x } \end{array} \right| = 8{/tex}, write the value of x.
question_text: If {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta } & { - x } & { 1 } \\ { \cos \theta } & { 1 } & { x } \end{array} \right| = 8{/tex} , write the value of x.
explanation: We have, {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta } & { - x } & { 1 } \\ { \cos \theta } & { 1 } & { x } \end{array} \right| = 8{/tex} On expanding along R 1 , we get {tex}x \left( - x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) - \sin \theta ( - x \sin \theta - \cos \theta ){/tex} {tex}+ \cos \theta ( - \sin \theta + x \cos \theta ) = 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - x ^ { 3 } - x + x \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin \theta \cos \theta - \sin \theta \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}+ x \cos ^ { 2 } \theta = 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - x ^ { 3 } - x + x \left( \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \right) = 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - x ^ { 3 } - x + x = 8{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - x ^ { 3 } = 8 \Rightarrow x ^ { 3 } + 8 = 0 \Rightarrow x ^ { 3 } + 2 ^ { 3 } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x + 2 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 - 2 x \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = - 2{/tex} {tex}\left[ : x ^ { 2 } - 2 x + 4 = 0\right.{/tex} , gives imaginary values {tex}]{/tex}
solution: We have, {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta } & { - x } & { 1 } \\ { \cos \theta } & { 1 } & { x } \end{array} \right| = 8{/tex} On expanding along R 1 , we get {tex}x \left( - x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) - \sin \theta ( - x \sin \theta - \cos \theta ){/tex} {tex}+ \cos \theta ( - \sin \theta + x \cos \theta ) = 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - x ^ { 3 } - x + x \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin \theta \cos \theta - \sin \theta \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}+ x \cos ^ { 2 } \theta = 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - x ^ { 3 } - x + x \left( \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \right) = 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - x ^ { 3 } - x + x = 8{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - x ^ { 3 } = 8 \Rightarrow x ^ { 3 } + 8 = 0 \Rightarrow x ^ { 3 } + 2 ^ { 3 } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x + 2 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 - 2 x \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = - 2{/tex} {tex}\left[ : x ^ { 2 } - 2 x + 4 = 0\right.{/tex} , gives imaginary values {tex}]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta } & { - x } & { 1 } \\ { \cos \theta } & { 1 } & { x } \end{array} \right| = 8{/tex}
On expanding along R1, we get
{tex}x \left( - x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) - \sin \theta ( - x \sin \theta - \cos \theta ){/tex}{tex}+ \cos \theta ( - \sin \theta + x \cos \theta ) = 8{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - x ^ { 3 } - x + x \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin \theta \cos \theta - \sin \theta \cos \theta{/tex}{tex}+ x \cos ^ { 2 } \theta = 8{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - x ^ { 3 } - x + x \left( \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \right) = 8{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - x ^ { 3 } - x + x = 8{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \because \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta = 1 \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - x ^ { 3 } = 8 \Rightarrow x ^ { 3 } + 8 = 0 \Rightarrow x ^ { 3 } + 2 ^ { 3 } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x + 2 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 - 2 x \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = - 2{/tex}
{tex}\left[ : x ^ { 2 } - 2 x + 4 = 0\right.{/tex}, gives imaginary values{tex}]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
For what values of k, the system of linear equations 
x+y+z=2
2x+y-z=3
3x+ 2y + kz = 4
has a unique solution?
question_text: For what values of k, the system of linear equations x+y+z=2 2x+y-z=3 3x+ 2y + kz = 4 has a unique solution?
solution_html:
According to the question, we have to find the value of the constant k for which  the  system of linear equations,
x+y+z=2
2x+y-z=3
3x + 2y+ kz = 4
has a unique solution.
It can be written in matrix form as
AX=B
where, A = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { - 1 } \\ { 3 } & { 2 } & { k } \end{array} \right] , X = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} \right]{/tex}and B = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { 2 } \\ { 3 } \\ { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
The condition for the system of linear equations has a unique solution, is
{tex}| A | \neq 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { - 1 } \\ { 3 } & { 2 } & { k } \end{array} \right| \neq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 (k + 2) - 1 (2k + 3) + 1 (4 - 3) {tex} \neq{/tex}0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k + 2 - 2k-3 +1 {tex} \neq{/tex}0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}- k{tex} \neq{/tex}0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}k \neq 0{/tex}
Hence, for a unique solution k should be a non-zero real number.

\frac { 1 } { 3 } } \\ { 1 } & { - \frac { 13 } { 3 } } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \\ { - 1 } & { 4 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Given system of equations can be written in matrix form as AX = B, where, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 8} & { 4 } & { 3 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 9 } & { 2 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right] , X = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} \right] \text { and } B = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 19 } \\ { 5 } \\ { 7 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { c } { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 0 + \frac { 10 } { 3 } - \frac { 7 } { 3 } } \\ { 19 - \frac { 65 } { 3 } + \frac { 14 } { 3 } } \\ { - 19 + 20 + 0 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} On comparing the corresponding elements, we get x = 1, y = 2 and z = 1.

\frac { 1 } { 3 } } \end{array} \right] A{/tex} On applying R 3 {tex}\leftrightarrow{/tex} R 1 , we get {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 8 } & { 4 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 0 } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } & { - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] A{/tex} On applying R 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R 3 - 4 R 2 , we get {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 0 } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } & { - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 1 } & { - 4 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] A{/tex} On applying R 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R 2 - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \\ { 0 } & { - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \\ { 1 } & { - 4 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] A{/tex} On applying R 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R 2 + R 3 , we get {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 0 } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } & { - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \\ { 1 } & { - \frac { 13 } { 3 } } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \\ { 1 } & { - 4 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] A{/tex} On applying R 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (-1) R 3 , we get {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 0 } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } & { - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \\ { 1 } & { - \frac { 13 } { 3 } } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \\ { - 1 } & { 4 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] A{/tex} Thus, {tex}A ^ { - 1 } = \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 0 } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } & { - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \\ { 1 } & { - \frac { 13 } { 3 } } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \\ { - 1 } & { 4 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Given system of equations can be written in matrix form as AX = B, where, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 8} & { 4 } & { 3 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 9 } & { 2 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right] , X = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} \right] \text { and } B = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 19 } \\ { 5 } \\ { 7 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { c } { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 0 + \frac { 10 } { 3 } - \frac { 7 } { 3 } } \\ { 19 - \frac { 65 } { 3 } + \frac { 14 } { 3 } } \\ { - 19 + 20 + 0 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} On comparing the corresponding elements, we get x = 1, y = 2 and z = 1.
solution_html:
Given matrix is A = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 8 } & { 4 } & { 3 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Here, we use elementary row transformations, so we consider
A = IA {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 8 } & { 4 } & { 3 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] A{/tex}
On applying {tex}R _ { 3 } \rightarrow R _ { 3 }{/tex}- 2R2, we get
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 8 } & { 4 } & { 3 } \\ { 23 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { - 3 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { - 2 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] A{/tex}
On applying R3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}- \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} R3, we get
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 8 } & { 4 } & { 3 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } & { - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \end{array} \right] A{/tex}
On applying R3 {tex}\leftrightarrow{/tex} R1, we get
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 8 } & { 4 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 0 } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } & { - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] A{/tex}
On applying R3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R3 - 4 R2, we get
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 0 } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } & { - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 1 } & { - 4 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] A{/tex}
On applying R2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R2 - 2R1, we get
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 0 } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } & { - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \\ { 0 } & { - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \\ { 1 } & { - 4 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] A{/tex}
On applying R2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R2 + R3, we get
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 0 } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } & { - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \\ { 1 } & { - \frac { 13 } { 3 } } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \\ { 1 } & { - 4 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] A{/tex}
On applying R3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}(-1) R3, we get
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 0 } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } & { - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \\ { 1 } & { - \frac { 13 } { 3 } } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \\ { - 1 } & { 4 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] A{/tex}
Thus, {tex}A ^ { - 1 } = \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 0 } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } & { - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \\ { 1 } & { - \frac { 13 } { 3 } } & { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \\ { - 1 } & { 4 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Given system of equations can be written in matrix form as
AX = B,
where, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 8} & { 4 } & { 3 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 9 } & { 2 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right] , X = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} \right] \text { and } B = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 19 } \\ { 5 } \\ { 7 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { c } { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 0 + \frac { 10 } { 3 } - \frac { 7 } { 3 } } \\ { 19 - \frac { 65 } { 3 } + \frac { 14 } { 3 } } \\ { - 19 + 20 + 0 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
On comparing the corresponding elements, we get
x = 1, y = 2 and z = 1.

question_html: A typist charges Rs145 for typing 10 English and 3 Hindi pages, while charges for typing 3 English and 10 Hindi pages are Rs 180. Using matrices, find the charges of typing 1 English and 1 Hindi page separately. However, typist charged only Rs 2 per page from a poor student Shyam for 5 Hindi pages. How much less was charged from this poor boy? Which values are reflected in this problem?
question_text: A typist charges Rs145 for typing 10 English and 3 Hindi pages, while charges for typing 3 English and 10 Hindi pages are Rs 180. Using matrices, find the charges of typing 1 English and 1 Hindi page separately. However, typist charged only Rs 2 per page from a poor student Shyam for 5 Hindi pages. How much less was charged from this poor boy? Which values are reflected in this problem?
explanation: Let the charges for each page of English and Hindi be Rs x and Rs y, respectively. Then, according to the given condition, we have 10x + 3y = 145 and 3x + 10y = 180 Above system of equations can be written in matrix form as AX = B.........(i) where, A = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { 3 } \\ { 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} , X = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \end{array} \right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { 145 } \\ { 180 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Now, |A| = {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { 3 } \\ { 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} = 100 - 9 = 91 {tex}\Rightarrow | A | \neq 0{/tex} , hence unique solution exists. Now, co-factors of the elements of |A| are C 1 1 = (-1) 2 10 =10 C 12 = (-1) 2 3 = -3 C 21 = (-1) 3 3 = -3 and C 2 2 = (-1) 4 10 = 10 {tex}\therefore \quad \operatorname { adj } ( A ) = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { C _ { 11 } } & { C _ { 12 } } \\ { C _ { 21 } } & { C _ { 22 } } \end{array} \right] ^ { T }{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { - 3 } \\ { - 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right] ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { - 3 } \\ { - 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Now, A -1 = {tex}\frac { 1 } { | A | } \operatorname { adj } ( A ) = \frac { 1 } { 91 } \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { - 3 } \\ { - 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} From Eq(i), we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = A -1 B {tex}\Rightarrow \quad X = \frac { 1 } { 91 } \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { - 3 } \\ { - 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 145 } \\ { 180 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 91 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 1450 - 540 } \\ { - 435 + 1800 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 91 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 910 } \\ { 1365 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 10 } \\ { 15 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} On equating the corresponding elements, we get x = 10 and y = 15 Hence, charges for each English page is Rs 10 and each Hindi page is Rs 15. Since, the typist charge only Rs 2 per Hindi page from a poor student Shyam. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of typing 5 Hindi pages for Shyam = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = Rs 10. But the original amount of typing 5 Hindi pages = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = Rs 75 Hence, the typist charge Rs 65(Rs 75- 10) less amount from poor boy. Care and concern towards weaker section of the society.
solution: Let the charges for each page of English and Hindi be Rs x and Rs y, respectively. Then, according to the given condition, we have 10x + 3y = 145 and 3x + 10y = 180 Above system of equations can be written in matrix form as AX = B.........(i) where, A = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { 3 } \\ { 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} , X = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \end{array} \right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { 145 } \\ { 180 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Now, |A| = {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { 3 } \\ { 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} = 100 - 9 = 91 {tex}\Rightarrow | A | \neq 0{/tex} , hence unique solution exists. Now, co-factors of the elements of |A| are C 1 1 = (-1) 2 10 =10 C 12 = (-1) 2 3 = -3 C 21 = (-1) 3 3 = -3 and C 2 2 = (-1) 4 10 = 10 {tex}\therefore \quad \operatorname { adj } ( A ) = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { C _ { 11 } } & { C _ { 12 } } \\ { C _ { 21 } } & { C _ { 22 } } \end{array} \right] ^ { T }{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { - 3 } \\ { - 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right] ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { - 3 } \\ { - 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Now, A -1 = {tex}\frac { 1 } { | A | } \operatorname { adj } ( A ) = \frac { 1 } { 91 } \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { - 3 } \\ { - 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} From Eq(i), we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = A -1 B {tex}\Rightarrow \quad X = \frac { 1 } { 91 } \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { - 3 } \\ { - 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 145 } \\ { 180 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 91 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 1450 - 540 } \\ { - 435 + 1800 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 91 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 910 } \\ { 1365 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 10 } \\ { 15 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} On equating the corresponding elements, we get x = 10 and y = 15 Hence, charges for each English page is Rs 10 and each Hindi page is Rs 15. Since, the typist charge only Rs 2 per Hindi page from a poor student Shyam. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of typing 5 Hindi pages for Shyam = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = Rs 10. But the original amount of typing 5 Hindi pages = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = Rs 75 Hence, the typist charge Rs 65(Rs 75- 10) less amount from poor boy. Care and concern towards weaker section of the society.
solution_html:
Let the charges for each page of English and Hindi be Rs x and Rs y, respectively.
Then, according to the given condition, we have
10x + 3y = 145
and 3x + 10y = 180
Above system of equations can be written in matrix form as
AX = B.........(i)
where, A = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { 3 } \\ { 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}, X = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \end{array} \right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { 145 } \\ { 180 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Now, |A| = {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { 3 } \\ { 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} = 100 - 9 = 91
{tex}\Rightarrow | A | \neq 0{/tex}, hence unique solution exists.
Now, co-factors of the elements of |A| are
C11 = (-1)2 10 =10
C12 = (-1)23 = -3
C21 = (-1)33 = -3
and C22 = (-1)4 10 = 10
{tex}\therefore \quad \operatorname { adj } ( A ) = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { C _ { 11 } } & { C _ { 12 } } \\ { C _ { 21 } } & { C _ { 22 } } \end{array} \right] ^ { T }{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { - 3 } \\ { - 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right] ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { - 3 } \\ { - 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Now, A-1 = {tex}\frac { 1 } { | A | } \operatorname { adj } ( A ) = \frac { 1 } { 91 } \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { - 3 } \\ { - 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
From Eq(i), we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = A-1B
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad X = \frac { 1 } { 91 } \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 10 } & { - 3 } \\ { - 3 } & { 10 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 145 } \\ { 180 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 91 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 1450 - 540 } \\ { - 435 + 1800 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 91 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 910 } \\ { 1365 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 10 } \\ { 15 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
On equating the corresponding elements, we get x = 10 and y = 15
Hence, charges for each English page is Rs 10 and each Hindi page is Rs 15.
Since, the typist charge only Rs 2 per Hindi page from a poor student Shyam.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of typing 5 Hindi pages for Shyam = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = Rs 10.
But the original amount of typing 5 Hindi pages = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = Rs 75
Hence, the typist charge Rs 65(Rs 75- 10) less amount from poor boy.
Care and concern towards weaker section of the society.

question_html: If x {tex}\in{/tex} N and {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 8, then find the value of x.
question_text: If x {tex}\in{/tex} N and {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 8, then find the value of x.
explanation: We are given that, {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 8 x {tex}\in{/tex} N We need to find the value of x. Determinant of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix is found as, So, take {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = [(x + 3) {tex}\times{/tex} 2x] - [(-2) {tex}\times{/tex} (-3x)] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 2x 2 + 6x - 6x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 2x 2 Since, {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 2 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = {tex}\frac{8}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm \sqrt{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex} 2 Since, x {tex}\in{/tex} N -2 is not a natural number. Thus, the value of x is 2
solution: We are given that, {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 8 x {tex}\in{/tex} N We need to find the value of x. Determinant of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix is found as, So, take {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = [(x + 3) {tex}\times{/tex} 2x] - [(-2) {tex}\times{/tex} (-3x)] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 2x 2 + 6x - 6x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 2x 2 Since, {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 2 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = {tex}\frac{8}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm \sqrt{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex} 2 Since, x {tex}\in{/tex} N -2 is not a natural number. Thus, the value of x is 2
solution_html:
We are given that,
{tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 8
x {tex}\in{/tex} N
We need to find the value of x.
Determinant of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix is found as,
So, take
{tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = [(x + 3) {tex}\times{/tex} 2x] - [(-2) {tex}\times{/tex} (-3x)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 2x2 + 6x - 6x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 2x2
Since, {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} x+3 & -2 \\ -3 x & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x2 = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = {tex}\frac{8}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm \sqrt{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex}2
Since, x {tex}\in{/tex} N
-2 is not a natural number.
Thus, the value of x is 2

question_html: A shopkeeper has 3 varieties of pens A, B and C. Meenu purchased 1 pen of each variety for a total of ₹ 21. Jean purchased 4 pens of A variety, 3 pens of B variety and 2 pens of C variety for ₹ 60. While Shikha purchased 6 pens of A variety, 2 pens of B variety and 3 pens of C variety for ₹ 70. Using matrix method find the cost of each pen.
question_text: A shopkeeper has 3 varieties of pens A, B and C. Meenu purchased 1 pen of each variety for a total of ₹ 21. Jean purchased 4 pens of A variety, 3 pens of B variety and 2 pens of C variety for ₹ 60. While Shikha purchased 6 pens of A variety, 2 pens of B variety and 3 pens of C variety for ₹ 70. Using matrix method find the cost of each pen.
explanation: Let the varieties of pen A, B and C be x, y and z respectively. According to question, x + y + z = 21 4x + 3y + 2z = 60 6x + 2y + 3z = 70 These three equations can be written as: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 6 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 21 \\ 60 \\ 70 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A X = B |A| = 1(9 – 4) – 1(12 – 12) + 1(8 – 18) = 1(5) – 1(0) + 1(– 10) = 5 – 0 – 10 = – 5 Hence, the unique solution given by x = A –1 B C 11 = (– 1) 1 + 1 (9 – 4) = 5 C 12 = (– 1) 1 + 2 (12 – 12) = 0 C 13 = (– 1) 1 + 3 (8 – 18) = – 10 C 21 = (– 1) 2 + 1 (3 – 2) = – 1 C 22 = (– 1) 2 + 2 (3 – 6) = – 3 C 23 = (– 1) 2 + 3 (2 – 6 ) = 4 C 31 = (– 1) 3 + 1 (2 – 3) = – 1 C 32 = (– 1) 3 + 2 (2 – 4) = 2 C 33 = (– 1) 3 + 3 (3 – 4) = – 1 {tex}\text { Adj } A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 0 & -10 \\ -1 & -3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \end{array}\right]^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ -10 & 4 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}x=A^{-1} B=\frac{1}{|A|}(\operatorname{adj} A) B{/tex} {tex}X=\frac{1}{-5}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ -10 & 4 & -1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 21 \\ 60 \\ 70 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{1}{-5}\left[\begin{array}{c} 105-60-70 \\ 0-180+140 \\ -210+240-70 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{1}{-5}\left[\begin{array}{c} 105-60-70 \\ 0-180+140 \\ -210+240-70 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{X} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{Z} \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-5}\left[\begin{array}{l} -25 \\ -40 \\ -40 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 5 \\ 8 \\ 8 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Hence, A = ₹5, B = ₹8 and C = ₹8
solution: Let the varieties of pen A, B and C be x, y and z respectively. According to question, x + y + z = 21 4x + 3y + 2z = 60 6x + 2y + 3z = 70 These three equations can be written as: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 6 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 21 \\ 60 \\ 70 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A X = B |A| = 1(9 – 4) – 1(12 – 12) + 1(8 – 18) = 1(5) – 1(0) + 1(– 10) = 5 – 0 – 10 = – 5 Hence, the unique solution given by x = A –1 B C 11 = (– 1) 1 + 1 (9 – 4) = 5 C 12 = (– 1) 1 + 2 (12 – 12) = 0 C 13 = (– 1) 1 + 3 (8 – 18) = – 10 C 21 = (– 1) 2 + 1 (3 – 2) = – 1 C 22 = (– 1) 2 + 2 (3 – 6) = – 3 C 23 = (– 1) 2 + 3 (2 – 6 ) = 4 C 31 = (– 1) 3 + 1 (2 – 3) = – 1 C 32 = (– 1) 3 + 2 (2 – 4) = 2 C 33 = (– 1) 3 + 3 (3 – 4) = – 1 {tex}\text { Adj } A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 0 & -10 \\ -1 & -3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \end{array}\right]^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ -10 & 4 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}x=A^{-1} B=\frac{1}{|A|}(\operatorname{adj} A) B{/tex} {tex}X=\frac{1}{-5}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ -10 & 4 & -1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 21 \\ 60 \\ 70 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{1}{-5}\left[\begin{array}{c} 105-60-70 \\ 0-180+140 \\ -210+240-70 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{1}{-5}\left[\begin{array}{c} 105-60-70 \\ 0-180+140 \\ -210+240-70 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{X} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{Z} \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-5}\left[\begin{array}{l} -25 \\ -40 \\ -40 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 5 \\ 8 \\ 8 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Hence, A = ₹5, B = ₹8 and C = ₹8
solution_html:
Let the varieties of pen A, B and C be x, y and z respectively.
According to question,
x + y + z = 21
4x + 3y + 2z = 60
6x + 2y + 3z = 70
These three equations can be written as:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 6 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 21 \\ 60 \\ 70 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
A X = B
|A| = 1(9 – 4) – 1(12 – 12) + 1(8 – 18)
= 1(5) – 1(0) + 1(– 10)
= 5 – 0 – 10
= – 5
Hence, the unique solution given by x = A–1B
C11 = (– 1)1 + 1 (9 – 4) = 5
C12 = (– 1)1 + 2 (12 – 12) = 0
C13 = (– 1)1 + 3 (8 – 18) = – 10
C21 = (– 1)2 + 1 (3 – 2) = – 1
C22 = (– 1)2 + 2 (3 – 6) = – 3
C23 = (– 1)2 + 3 (2 – 6 ) = 4
C31 = (– 1)3 + 1 (2 – 3) = – 1
C32 = (– 1)3 + 2 (2 – 4) = 2
C33 = (– 1)3 + 3 (3 – 4) = – 1
{tex}\text { Adj } A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 0 & -10 \\ -1 & -3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \end{array}\right]^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ -10 & 4 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}x=A^{-1} B=\frac{1}{|A|}(\operatorname{adj} A) B{/tex}
{tex}X=\frac{1}{-5}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ -10 & 4 & -1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 21 \\ 60 \\ 70 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}x=\frac{1}{-5}\left[\begin{array}{c} 105-60-70 \\ 0-180+140 \\ -210+240-70 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}x=\frac{1}{-5}\left[\begin{array}{c} 105-60-70 \\ 0-180+140 \\ -210+240-70 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{X} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{Z} \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-5}\left[\begin{array}{l} -25 \\ -40 \\ -40 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 5 \\ 8 \\ 8 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Hence, A = ₹5, B = ₹8 and C = ₹8

question_text: Prove that: {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { a ^ { 2 } } & { b c } & { a c + c ^ { 2 } } \\ { a ^ { 2 } + a b } & { b ^ { 2 } } & { a c } \\ { a b } & { b ^ { 2 } + b c } & { c ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right| = 4 a ^ { 2 } b ^ { 2 } c ^ { 2 }{/tex}

To prove {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { a ^ { 2 } } & { b } & { a c + c ^ { 2 } } \\ { a ^ { 2 } + a b } & { b ^ { 2 } } & { a c } \\ { a b } & { b ^ { 2 } + b c } & { c ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} = 4a2b2c2
Let LHS = {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { a ^ { 2 } } & { b c } & { a c + c ^ { 2 } } \\ { a ^ { 2 } + a b } & { b ^ { 2 } } & { a c } \\ { a b } & { b ^ { 2 } + b c } & { c ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
Therefore, on taking a common from C1, b from C2 and c from C3, we get,
LHS = abc{tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { a } & { c } & { a + c } \\ { a + b } & { b } & { a } \\ { b } & { b + c } & { c } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
Therefore,on applying C1{tex} \rightarrow{/tex} C1 + C2 - C3, we get,
LHS = abc{tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 0 } & { c } & { a + c } \\ { 2 b } & { b } & { a } \\ { 2 b } & { b + c } & { c } \end{array} \right|{/tex}

question_html: If, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] \text { and } B = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { - 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} write the value of |AB|.
question_text: If, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] \text { and } B = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { - 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} write the value of |AB|.
explanation: If, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] \text { and } B = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { - 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} then,we have to find the value of |AB|. Clearly, {tex}| A | = \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right| = - 1 - 6 = - 7{/tex} and {tex}| B | = \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { - 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| = 1 + 3 = 4{/tex} {tex}\therefore | A B | = | A | \cdot | B | = ( - 7 \times4) = - 28{/tex}
solution: If, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] \text { and } B = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { - 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} then,we have to find the value of |AB|. Clearly, {tex}| A | = \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right| = - 1 - 6 = - 7{/tex} and {tex}| B | = \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { - 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| = 1 + 3 = 4{/tex} {tex}\therefore | A B | = | A | \cdot | B | = ( - 7 \times4) = - 28{/tex}
solution_html:
If, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] \text { and } B = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { - 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} then,we have to find the value of |AB|.

Clearly, {tex}| A | = \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right| = - 1 - 6 = - 7{/tex}
and {tex}| B | = \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { - 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| = 1 + 3 = 4{/tex}
{tex}\therefore | A B | = | A | \cdot | B | = ( - 7 \times4) = - 28{/tex}

question_html: In the interval  {tex}\frac{{tex}\pi{/tex}}{2}{/tex} < x <{tex}\pi{/tex}, find the value of x for which the matrix {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 \sin x } & { 3 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 \sin x } \end{array} \right]{/tex} is singular.
question_text: In the interval {tex}\frac{ {tex}\pi{/tex} }{2}{/tex} < x < {tex}\pi{/tex} , find the value of x for which the matrix {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 \sin x } & { 3 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 \sin x } \end{array} \right]{/tex} is singular.
explanation: Let {tex} A = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 \sin x } & { 3 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 \sin x } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex} \because{/tex} A is a singular matrix. {tex}\therefore \quad | A | = 0 \Rightarrow \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 2 \sin x } & { 3 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 \sin x } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 \sin ^ { 2 } x - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow \sin ^2 x = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin x = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 }{/tex} ​​​​​​ {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { taking positive square } } \\ { \text { root because } \frac { \pi } { 2 } < x < \pi } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad x = \frac { 2 \pi } { 3 }{/tex}
solution: Let {tex} A = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 \sin x } & { 3 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 \sin x } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex} \because{/tex} A is a singular matrix. {tex}\therefore \quad | A | = 0 \Rightarrow \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 2 \sin x } & { 3 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 \sin x } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 \sin ^ { 2 } x - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow \sin ^2 x = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin x = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 }{/tex} ​​​​​​ {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { taking positive square } } \\ { \text { root because } \frac { \pi } { 2 } < x < \pi } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad x = \frac { 2 \pi } { 3 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex} A = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 \sin x } & { 3 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 \sin x } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \because{/tex} A is a singular matrix.
{tex}\therefore \quad | A | = 0 \Rightarrow \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 2 \sin x } & { 3 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 \sin x } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 \sin ^ { 2 } x - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow \sin ^2 x = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin x = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 }{/tex} ​​​​​​{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { taking positive square } } \\ { \text { root because } \frac { \pi } { 2 } < x < \pi } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad x = \frac { 2 \pi } { 3 }{/tex}

(i) {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} and, 4 A - 3I = 4 {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right] - 3 \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 8 } & { - 4 } \\ { - 4 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right] - \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 3 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 5 } & { - 4 } \\ { - 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} ...(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get A 2 = 4A- 3I ...(iii) Hence proved. Here, {tex} | A | = \left| \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right| = 4 - 1 = 3 \neq 0{/tex} Therefore, A is non-singular matrix and its inverse exists. Now, pre-multiplying both sides of Eq. (iii) by A -1 , we get A -1 .A 2 = A -1 .(4A- 3I) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} (A -1 . A). A= 4A -1 . A - 3A -1 .I {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} IA= 4I - 3A -1 [ {tex} \because {/tex} A . A -1 = I=A -1 A andA -1I =A -1 ] {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} A= 4I - 3A -1 [ {tex} \because {/tex} IA= A =AI] {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3A -1 = 4I-A {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} A -1 = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left( 4 \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]\right.{/tex} - {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left( \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 4 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] - \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right] \right){/tex} {tex}{/tex} Therefore, {tex} A ^ { - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 / 3 } & { 1 / 3 } \\ { 1 / 3 } & { 2 / 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{/tex}According to the question,we are given that ,A = {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} and I is the identity matrix of order 2, then we have to show that A2 = 4A- 3I. Hence,we  need to find A{tex}^{-1}{/tex}.

Now, A2 = A A= {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
= {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 4 + 1 } & { - 2 - 2 } \\ { - 2 - 2 } & { 1 + 4 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 5 } & { - 4 } \\ { - 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
[multiplying row by column] ... (i)
{tex}{/tex}
 {tex}{/tex}and, 4 A - 3I = 4{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right] - 3 \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 8 } & { - 4 } \\ { - 4 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right] - \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 3 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 5 } & { - 4 } \\ { - 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}...(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
A2 = 4A- 3I  ...(iii)
Hence proved.
Here, {tex} | A | = \left| \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right| = 4 - 1 = 3 \neq 0{/tex}
Therefore, A is non-singular matrix and its inverse exists.
Now, pre-multiplying both sides of Eq. (iii) by A-1, we get
A-1.A2 = A-1 .(4A- 3I)
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}(A-1. A). A= 4A-1 . A - 3A-1.I
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} IA= 4I - 3A-1
[{tex} \because {/tex} A . A-1 = I=A-1 A andA-1I=A-1]
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} A= 4I - 3A-1 [{tex} \because {/tex}IA= A =AI]
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}3A-1 = 4I-A
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} A-1 = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left( 4 \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]\right.{/tex}- {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
= {tex} \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left( \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 4 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] - \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right] \right){/tex}
{tex}{/tex} Therefore,{tex} A ^ { - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 / 3 } & { 1 / 3 } \\ { 1 / 3 } & { 2 / 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}

question_html: If A = {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 } & { 3 } \\ { 1 } & { - 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}, then verify that A adj A {tex}=|\mathrm{A}|I{/tex}. Also find A-1.
question_text: If A = {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 } & { 3 } \\ { 1 } & { - 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} , then verify that A adj A {tex}=|\mathrm{A}|I{/tex} . Also find A -1 .
explanation: We have {tex}|A|={/tex} = 1 (16 – 9) –3 (4 – 3) + 3 (3 – 4) = 1 {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 Now A 11 = 7, A 12 = –1, A 13 = –1, A 21 = –3, A 22 = 1, A 23 = 0, A 31 = –3, A 32 = 0, A 33 = 1 Therefore {tex}\text {adj} \mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now {tex}\mathrm{A}(a d j \mathrm{A})=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7-3-3 & -3+3+0 & -3+0+3 \\ 7-4-3 & -3+4+0 & -3+0+3 \\ 7-3-4 & -3+3+0 & -3+0+4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=(1) \quad\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=|A| \cdot 1{/tex} Also {tex}\mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{|\mathrm{A}|} a d j \mathrm{A}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{1}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} . Which is the required solution.
solution: We have {tex}|A|={/tex} = 1 (16 – 9) –3 (4 – 3) + 3 (3 – 4) = 1 {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 Now A 11 = 7, A 12 = –1, A 13 = –1, A 21 = –3, A 22 = 1, A 23 = 0, A 31 = –3, A 32 = 0, A 33 = 1 Therefore {tex}\text {adj} \mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now {tex}\mathrm{A}(a d j \mathrm{A})=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7-3-3 & -3+3+0 & -3+0+3 \\ 7-4-3 & -3+4+0 & -3+0+3 \\ 7-3-4 & -3+3+0 & -3+0+4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=(1) \quad\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=|A| \cdot 1{/tex} Also {tex}\mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{|\mathrm{A}|} a d j \mathrm{A}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{1}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} . Which is the required solution.
solution_html:
We have {tex}|A|={/tex} = 1 (16 – 9) –3 (4 – 3) + 3 (3 – 4) = 1 {tex}\neq{/tex} 0
Now A11 = 7, A12 = –1, A13 = –1, A21 = –3, A22 = 1, A23 = 0, A31 = –3, A32 = 0, A33 = 1
Therefore {tex}\text {adj} \mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now {tex}\mathrm{A}(a d j \mathrm{A})=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7-3-3 & -3+3+0 & -3+0+3 \\ 7-4-3 & -3+4+0 & -3+0+3 \\ 7-3-4 & -3+3+0 & -3+0+4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=(1) \quad\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=|A| \cdot 1{/tex}
Also {tex}\mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{|\mathrm{A}|} a d j \mathrm{A}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{1}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}.

Which is the required solution.
