question_html: Solve the equation: {tex}\Delta = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x + a}&x&x \\ x&{x + a}&x \\ x&x&{x + a} \end{array}} \right| = 0,a \ne 0{/tex}
question_text: Solve the equation: {tex}\Delta = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x + a}&x&x \\ x&{x + a}&x \\ x&x&{x + a} \end{array}} \right| = 0,a \ne 0{/tex}
explanation: Given: {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x + a}&x&x \\ x&{x + a}&x \\ x&x&{x + a} \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex} Applying : {tex}\left[ {{R_1} \to {R_1} + {R_2} + {R_3}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3x + a}&{3x + a}&{3x + a} \\ x&{x + a}&x \\ x&x&{x + a} \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex} Take (3x+a) common from first row {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {3x + a} \right)\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&1&1 \\ x&{x + a}&x \\ x&x&{x + a} \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex} Either 3x + a = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{{ - a}}{3}{/tex} ….(i) Or {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&1&1 \\ x&{x + a}&x \\ x&x&{x + a} \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ {{C_2} \to {C_2} - {C_1}\,\,and\,\,{C_3} \to {C_3} - {C_1}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ x&a&0 \\ x&0&a \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex} Expanding along Ist row {tex}\Rightarrow 1\left( {{a^2} - 0} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {a^2} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow a = 0{/tex} But this is contrary as given that {tex}a \ne 0{/tex} . Therefore, from eq. (i), {tex}x = \frac{{ - a}}{3}{/tex} is only the solution.
solution: Given: {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x + a}&x&x \\ x&{x + a}&x \\ x&x&{x + a} \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex} Applying : {tex}\left[ {{R_1} \to {R_1} + {R_2} + {R_3}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3x + a}&{3x + a}&{3x + a} \\ x&{x + a}&x \\ x&x&{x + a} \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex} Take (3x+a) common from first row {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {3x + a} \right)\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&1&1 \\ x&{x + a}&x \\ x&x&{x + a} \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex} Either 3x + a = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{{ - a}}{3}{/tex} ….(i) Or {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&1&1 \\ x&{x + a}&x \\ x&x&{x + a} \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ {{C_2} \to {C_2} - {C_1}\,\,and\,\,{C_3} \to {C_3} - {C_1}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ x&a&0 \\ x&0&a \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex} Expanding along Ist row {tex}\Rightarrow 1\left( {{a^2} - 0} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {a^2} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow a = 0{/tex} But this is contrary as given that {tex}a \ne 0{/tex} . Therefore, from eq. (i), {tex}x = \frac{{ - a}}{3}{/tex} is only the solution.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x + a}&x&x \\ x&{x + a}&x \\ x&x&{x + a} \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex}

Applying : {tex}\left[ {{R_1} \to {R_1} + {R_2} + {R_3}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3x + a}&{3x + a}&{3x + a} \\ x&{x + a}&x \\ x&x&{x + a} \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex}

Take (3x+a) common from first row

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {3x + a} \right)\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&1&1 \\ x&{x + a}&x \\ x&x&{x + a} \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex}

Either 3x + a = 0

{tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{{ - a}}{3}{/tex}….(i)

Or {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&1&1 \\ x&{x + a}&x \\ x&x&{x + a} \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex}

{tex}\left[ {{C_2} \to {C_2} - {C_1}\,\,and\,\,{C_3} \to {C_3} - {C_1}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ x&a&0 \\ x&0&a \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex}

Expanding along Ist row

{tex}\Rightarrow 1\left( {{a^2} - 0} \right) = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow {a^2} = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow a = 0{/tex}

But this is contrary as given that {tex}a \ne 0{/tex}.

Therefore, from eq. (i), {tex}x = \frac{{ - a}}{3}{/tex} is only the solution.

question_html:
Using matrix method, solve the following system of equations
{tex}\frac { 2 } { x } + \frac { 3 } { y } + \frac { 10 } { z } = 4{/tex}
{tex}\frac { 4 } { x } - \frac { 6 } { y } + \frac { 5 } { z } = 1{/tex}
and {tex}\frac { 6 } { x } + \frac { 9 } { y } - \frac { 20 } { z } = 2 ,where\ x , y , z \neq 0{/tex}.
question_text: Using matrix method, solve the following system of equations {tex}\frac { 2 } { x } + \frac { 3 } { y } + \frac { 10 } { z } = 4{/tex} {tex}\frac { 4 } { x } - \frac { 6 } { y } + \frac { 5 } { z } = 1{/tex} and {tex}\frac { 6 } { x } + \frac { 9 } { y } - \frac { 20 } { z } = 2 ,where\ x , y , z \neq 0{/tex} .
explanation: Given system of equations is {tex}\frac { 2 } { x } + \frac { 3 } { y } + \frac { 10 } { z } = 4 , \frac { 4 } { x } - \frac { 6 } { y } + \frac { 5 } { z } = 1{/tex} and {tex}\frac { 6 } { x } + \frac { 9 } { y } - \frac { 20 } { z } = 2 ; x , y , z \neq 0{/tex} We shall make use of substitutions in order to get the required result. Let {tex}\frac { 1 } { x } = u , \frac { 1 } { y } = v \text { and } \frac { 1 } { z } = w{/tex} , then given system of equations reduces to , {tex}\left. \begin{array} { r } { 2 u + 3 v + 10 w = 4 } \\ { 4 u - 6 v + 5 w = 1 } \\ { \text { and } \quad 6 u + 9 v - 20 w = 2 } \end{array} \right\}{/tex} ...........(i) Therefore,Above system of Eqs. (i) can be written in matrix form as AX = B, where, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 2 } & { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 4 } & { - 6 } & { 5 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right] B = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 4 } \\ { 1 } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right] \text { and } X = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { u } \\ { v } \\ { w } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Its solution is given by X = A - 1 B Here, |A| = 2 (120 - 45) - 3 (-80 - 30) + 10 (36 + 36) = 2(75) -3 (-110) + 10(72) = 150 + 330 + 720= 1200 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A|= 1200 Since, |A| {tex}\neq {/tex} 0, so unique solution exists. Now, cofactors of elements of |A| are, {tex}A _ { 11 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } \left| \begin{array} { r r } { - 6 } & { 5 } \\ { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right| = 1 ( 120 - 45 ) = 75{/tex} {tex}A _ { 12 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 3 } \left| \begin{array} { r r } { 4 } & { 5 } \\ { 6 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right| = - 1 ( - 80 - 30 ) = 110{/tex} {tex}A _ { 13 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 4 } \left| \begin{array} { r r } { 4 } & { - 6 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } \end{array} \right| = 1 ( 36 + 36 ) = 72{/tex} {tex}A _ { 21 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 3 } \left| \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right| = - 1 ( - 60 - 90 ) = 150{/tex} {tex}A _ { 22 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 4 } \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 2 } & { 10 } \\ { 6 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right| = 1 ( - 40 - 60 ) = - 100{/tex} {tex}A _ { 23 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 5 } \left| \begin{array} { l l } { 2 } & { 3 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } \end{array} \right| = - 1 ( 18 - 18 ) = 0{/tex} {tex}A _ { 31 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 4 } \left| \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { - 6 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right| = 1 ( 15 + 60 ) = 75{/tex} {tex}A _ { 32 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 5 } \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 2 } & { 10 } \\ { 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right| = - 1 ( 10 - 40 ) = 30{/tex} {tex}A _ { 33 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 6 } \left| \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { 3 } \\ { 4 } & { - 6 } \end{array} \right| = 1 ( - 12 - 12 ) = - 24{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \operatorname { adj } ( A ) = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { A _ { 11 } } & { A _ { 12 } } & { A _ { 13 } } \\ { A _ { 21 } } & { A _ { 22 } } & { A _ { 23 } } \\ { A _ { 31 } } & { A _ { 32 } } & { A _ { 33 } } \end{array} \right] ^ { T }{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 75 } & { 110 } & { 72 } \\ { 150 } & { - 100 } & { 0 } \\ { 75 } & { 30 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right] ^ { T }{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 75 } & { 150 } & { 75 } \\ { 110 } & { - 100 } & { 30 } \\ { 72 } & { 0 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} and {tex}A ^ { - 1 } = \frac { \operatorname { adj } ( A ) } { | A | } = \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 75 } & { 150 } & { 75 } \\ { 110 } & { - 100 } & { 30 } \\ { 72 } & { 0 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Therefore,on putting the values of X, A -1 and B in eq. (ii), we get, {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { u } \\ { v } \\ { w } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 75 } & { 150 } & { 75 } \\ { 110 } & { - 100 } & { 30 } \\ { 72 } & { 0 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 4 } \\ { 1 } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 300 + 150 + 150 } \\ { 440 - 100 + 60 } \\ { 288 + 0 - 48 } \end{array} \right] \Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { l } { u } \\ { v } \\ { w } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 600 } \\ { 400 } \\ { 240 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Therefore,on comparing corresponding elements, we get, {tex}u = \frac { 600 } { 1200 } , v = \frac { 400 } { 1200 } , w = \frac { 240 } { 1200 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , v = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \text { and } w = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex} But {tex}\frac { 1 } { x } = u , \frac { 1 } { y } = v \text { and } \frac { 1 } { z } = w{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { x } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , \frac { 1 } { y } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \text { and } \frac { 1 } { z } = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 2, y = 3 and z = 5

solution_html:
Given system of equations is
{tex}\frac { 2 } { x } + \frac { 3 } { y } + \frac { 10 } { z } = 4 , \frac { 4 } { x } - \frac { 6 } { y } + \frac { 5 } { z } = 1{/tex}
and {tex}\frac { 6 } { x } + \frac { 9 } { y } - \frac { 20 } { z } = 2 ; x , y , z \neq 0{/tex}

We shall make use of substitutions in order to get the required  result.
Let {tex}\frac { 1 } { x } = u , \frac { 1 } { y } = v \text { and } \frac { 1 } { z } = w{/tex},
then  given system of equations reduces to ,
{tex}\left. \begin{array} { r } { 2 u + 3 v + 10 w = 4 } \\ { 4 u - 6 v + 5 w = 1 } \\ { \text { and } \quad 6 u + 9 v - 20 w = 2 } \end{array} \right\}{/tex}

...........(i)
Therefore,Above system of Eqs. (i) can be written in matrix form as AX = B, where,
{tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 2 } & { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 4 } & { - 6 } & { 5 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right] B = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 4 } \\ { 1 } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right] \text { and } X = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { u } \\ { v } \\ { w } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Its solution is given by
X = A- 1B
Here, |A| = 2 (120 - 45) - 3 (-80 - 30) + 10 (36 + 36)
= 2(75) -3 (-110) + 10(72)
= 150 + 330 + 720= 1200
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A|= 1200
Since, |A|{tex}\neq {/tex} 0, so unique solution exists. Now, cofactors of elements of |A| are,
{tex}A _ { 11 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } \left| \begin{array} { r r } { - 6 } & { 5 } \\ { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right| = 1 ( 120 - 45 ) = 75{/tex}
{tex}A _ { 12 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 3 } \left| \begin{array} { r r } { 4 } & { 5 } \\ { 6 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right| = - 1 ( - 80 - 30 ) = 110{/tex}
{tex}A _ { 13 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 4 } \left| \begin{array} { r r } { 4 } & { - 6 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } \end{array} \right| = 1 ( 36 + 36 ) = 72{/tex}
{tex}A _ { 21 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 3 } \left| \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 9 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right| = - 1 ( - 60 - 90 ) = 150{/tex}
{tex}A _ { 22 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 4 } \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 2 } & { 10 } \\ { 6 } & { - 20 } \end{array} \right| = 1 ( - 40 - 60 ) = - 100{/tex}
{tex}A _ { 23 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 5 } \left| \begin{array} { l l } { 2 } & { 3 } \\ { 6 } & { 9 } \end{array} \right| = - 1 ( 18 - 18 ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}A _ { 31 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 4 } \left| \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 10 } \\ { - 6 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right| = 1 ( 15 + 60 ) = 75{/tex}
{tex}A _ { 32 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 5 } \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 2 } & { 10 } \\ { 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right| = - 1 ( 10 - 40 ) = 30{/tex}
{tex}A _ { 33 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { 6 } \left| \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { 3 } \\ { 4 } & { - 6 } \end{array} \right| = 1 ( - 12 - 12 ) = - 24{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \operatorname { adj } ( A ) = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { A _ { 11 } } & { A _ { 12 } } & { A _ { 13 } } \\ { A _ { 21 } } & { A _ { 22 } } & { A _ { 23 } } \\ { A _ { 31 } } & { A _ { 32 } } & { A _ { 33 } } \end{array} \right] ^ { T }{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 75 } & { 110 } & { 72 } \\ { 150 } & { - 100 } & { 0 } \\ { 75 } & { 30 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right] ^ { T }{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 75 } & { 150 } & { 75 } \\ { 110 } & { - 100 } & { 30 } \\ { 72 } & { 0 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
and {tex}A ^ { - 1 } = \frac { \operatorname { adj } ( A ) } { | A | } = \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 75 } & { 150 } & { 75 } \\ { 110 } & { - 100 } & { 30 } \\ { 72 } & { 0 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Therefore,on putting the values of X, A-1 and B in eq. (ii),
we get,
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { u } \\ { v } \\ { w } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 75 } & { 150 } & { 75 } \\ { 110 } & { - 100 } & { 30 } \\ { 72 } & { 0 } & { - 24 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 4 } \\ { 1 } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 300 + 150 + 150 } \\ { 440 - 100 + 60 } \\ { 288 + 0 - 48 } \end{array} \right] \Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { l } { u } \\ { v } \\ { w } \end{array} \right] = \frac { 1 } { 1200 } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 600 } \\ { 400 } \\ { 240 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Therefore,on comparing corresponding elements, we get,
{tex}u = \frac { 600 } { 1200 } , v = \frac { 400 } { 1200 } , w = \frac { 240 } { 1200 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , v = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \text { and } w = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex}
But {tex}\frac { 1 } { x } = u , \frac { 1 } { y } = v \text { and } \frac { 1 } { z } = w{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { x } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , \frac { 1 } { y } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \text { and } \frac { 1 } { z } = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 2, y = 3 and z = 5

question_html: Evaluate the determinant {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c } { \cos 15 ^ { \circ } } & { \sin 15 ^ { \circ } } \\ { \sin 75 ^ { \circ } } & { \cos 75 ^ { \circ } } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate the determinant {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c } { \cos 15 ^ { \circ } } & { \sin 15 ^ { \circ } } \\ { \sin 75 ^ { \circ } } & { \cos 75 ^ { \circ } } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}\Delta = \left| \begin{array} { l l } { \cos 15 ^ { \circ } } & { \sin 15{\circ} } \\ { \sin 75 ^ { \circ } } & { \cos 75 ^ { \circ } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} On expanding, we get {tex}\Delta = \left( \cos 15 ^ { \circ } \cos 75 ^ { \circ } - \sin 15 ^ { \circ } \sin 75 ^ { \circ } \right){/tex} = cos (15° + 75°) [ {tex} \because {/tex} cos x cos y - sin x sin y = cos (x + y] = cos 90° = 0 {tex}\left[ \because \cos 90 ^ { \circ } = 0 \right]{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\Delta = \left| \begin{array} { l l } { \cos 15 ^ { \circ } } & { \sin 15{\circ} } \\ { \sin 75 ^ { \circ } } & { \cos 75 ^ { \circ } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} On expanding, we get {tex}\Delta = \left( \cos 15 ^ { \circ } \cos 75 ^ { \circ } - \sin 15 ^ { \circ } \sin 75 ^ { \circ } \right){/tex} = cos (15° + 75°) [ {tex} \because {/tex} cos x cos y - sin x sin y = cos (x + y] = cos 90° = 0 {tex}\left[ \because \cos 90 ^ { \circ } = 0 \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let{tex}\Delta = \left| \begin{array} { l l } { \cos 15 ^ { \circ } } & { \sin 15{\circ} } \\ { \sin 75 ^ { \circ } } & { \cos 75 ^ { \circ } } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
On expanding, we get
{tex}\Delta = \left( \cos 15 ^ { \circ } \cos 75 ^ { \circ } - \sin 15 ^ { \circ } \sin 75 ^ { \circ } \right){/tex}
= cos (15° + 75°)
[{tex} \because {/tex}cos x cos y - sin x sin y = cos (x + y]
= cos 90° = 0 {tex}\left[ \because \cos 90 ^ { \circ } = 0 \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: For what value of x is the matrix {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 6-x & 4 \\ 3-x & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} singular?
question_text: For what value of x is the matrix {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 6-x & 4 \\ 3-x & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} singular?
explanation: The given matrix is {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 6-x & ~4 \\ 3-x & ~1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We need to find the value of x such that the matrix is singular. i.e, {tex}\begin{vmatrix}6-x & ~4 \\ 3-x & ~1 \end{vmatrix}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (6 - x) {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} (3 - x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (6 - x) - (12 - 4x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6 - x - 12 + 4x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x - x - 12 + 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x - 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{6}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 Thus, the value of x = 2 for which the matrix is singular. x = 2
solution: The given matrix is {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 6-x & ~4 \\ 3-x & ~1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We need to find the value of x such that the matrix is singular. i.e, {tex}\begin{vmatrix}6-x & ~4 \\ 3-x & ~1 \end{vmatrix}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (6 - x) {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} (3 - x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (6 - x) - (12 - 4x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6 - x - 12 + 4x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x - x - 12 + 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x - 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{6}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 Thus, the value of x = 2 for which the matrix is singular. x = 2
solution_html:
The given matrix is
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 6-x & ~4 \\ 3-x & ~1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
We need to find the value of x such that the matrix is singular.
i.e, {tex}\begin{vmatrix}6-x & ~4 \\ 3-x & ~1 \end{vmatrix}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (6 - x) {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} (3 - x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (6 - x) - (12 - 4x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6 - x - 12 + 4x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x - x - 12 + 6 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x - 6 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{6}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2
Thus, the value of x = 2 for which the matrix is singular. x = 2

question_html:
Use product {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 0&2&{ - 3} \\ 3&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0&1 \\ 9&2&{ - 3} \\ 6&1&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} to solve the system of equations:
x - y + 2z = 1;
2y - 3z = 1;
3x - 2y + 4z = 2.
question_text: Use product {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 0&2&{ - 3} \\ 3&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0&1 \\ 9&2&{ - 3} \\ 6&1&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} to solve the system of equations: x - y + 2z = 1; 2y - 3z = 1; 3x - 2y + 4z = 2.
explanation: {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 0&2&{ - 3} \\ 3&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0&1 \\ 9&2&{ - 3} \\ 6&1&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right] {/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 0&2&{ - 3} \\ 3&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right]^{ - 1}} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0&1 \\ 9&2&{ - 3} \\ 6&1&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} The given system of equations can be rewritten as, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 0&2&{ - 3} \\ 3&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right] \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] ={\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 0&2&{ - 3} \\ 3&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right]^{ - 1}} \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] ={\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} -2&{ 0}&1\\ 9&2&{ - 3} \\ 6&{ 1}&-2\end{array}} \right]{ }} \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0 \\ 5 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} x = 0, y = 5, z = 3
solution: {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 0&2&{ - 3} \\ 3&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0&1 \\ 9&2&{ - 3} \\ 6&1&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right] {/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 0&2&{ - 3} \\ 3&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right]^{ - 1}} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0&1 \\ 9&2&{ - 3} \\ 6&1&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} The given system of equations can be rewritten as, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 0&2&{ - 3} \\ 3&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right] \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] ={\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 0&2&{ - 3} \\ 3&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right]^{ - 1}} \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] ={\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} -2&{ 0}&1\\ 9&2&{ - 3} \\ 6&{ 1}&-2\end{array}} \right]{ }} \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0 \\ 5 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} x = 0, y = 5, z = 3
solution_html:
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 0&2&{ - 3} \\ 3&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0&1 \\ 9&2&{ - 3} \\ 6&1&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right] {/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 0&2&{ - 3} \\ 3&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right]^{ - 1}} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0&1 \\ 9&2&{ - 3} \\ 6&1&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

The given system of equations can be rewritten as,
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 0&2&{ - 3} \\ 3&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right] \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] ={\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 0&2&{ - 3} \\ 3&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right]^{ - 1}} \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] ={\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} -2&{ 0}&1\\ 9&2&{ - 3} \\ 6&{ 1}&-2\end{array}} \right]{ }} \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0 \\ 5 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
x = 0, y = 5, z = 3

question_html: For what value of x, the matrix {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5-x & x+1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} is singular?
question_text: For what value of x, the matrix {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5-x & x+1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} is singular?
explanation: Here A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5-x & x+1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Hence, |A| = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 5-x & x+1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} =(5 - x) {tex}\times{/tex} 4 - (x + 1) {tex}\times{/tex} 2 ...(Expanding along R 1 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A| = 18 - 6x For A to be a singular matrix, |A| has to be 0. Therefore, 18 - 6x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3.
solution: Here A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5-x & x+1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Hence, |A| = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 5-x & x+1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} =(5 - x) {tex}\times{/tex} 4 - (x + 1) {tex}\times{/tex} 2 ...(Expanding along R 1 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A| = 18 - 6x For A to be a singular matrix, |A| has to be 0. Therefore, 18 - 6x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3.
solution_html:
Here
A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5-x & x+1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
Hence, |A| = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 5-x & x+1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} 
=(5 - x) {tex}\times{/tex} 4 - (x + 1) {tex}\times{/tex} 2   ...(Expanding along R1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A| = 18 - 6x
For A to be a singular matrix, |A| has to be 0.
Therefore, 18 - 6x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3.

question_html:
Solve the system of the following equations: (Using matrices):

{tex}\frac{2}{x} + \frac{3}{y} + \frac{{10}}{z} = 4;{/tex}{tex}\frac{4}{x} - \frac{6}{y} + \frac{5}{z} = 1;{/tex}{tex}\frac{6}{x} + \frac{9}{y} - \frac{{20}}{z} = 2;{/tex}
question_text: Solve the system of the following equations: (Using matrices): {tex}\frac{2}{x} + \frac{3}{y} + \frac{{10}}{z} = 4;{/tex} {tex}\frac{4}{x} - \frac{6}{y} + \frac{5}{z} = 1;{/tex} {tex}\frac{6}{x} + \frac{9}{y} - \frac{{20}}{z} = 2;{/tex}
explanation: Put {tex}\frac{1}{x} = u,\frac{1}{y} = v{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{z} = w{/tex} in the given equations, {tex}2u + 3v + 10w = 4;{/tex} {tex}4u - 6v + 5w = 1;{/tex} {tex}6u + 9v - 20w = 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The matrix form of given equations is {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&{10} \\ 4&{ - 6}&5 \\ 6&9&{ - 20} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ v \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} [AX= B] Here, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&{10} \\ 4&{ - 6}&5 \\ 6&9&{ - 20} \end{array}} \right],X\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ v \\ z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left| A \right| = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&{10} \\ 4&{ - 6}&5 \\ 6&9&{ - 20} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= 2\left( {120 - 45} \right) - 3\left( { - 80 - 30} \right) + 10\left( {36 + 36} \right){/tex} {tex} = 150 + 330 + 750 = 1200 \ne 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore {A^{ - 1}}{/tex} exists and unique solution is {tex}X = {A^{ - 1}}B{/tex} ….(i) Now {tex}{A_{11}} = 75,{A_{12}} = 110,{A_{13}} = 72{/tex} and {tex}{A_{21}} = 150,{A_{22}} = - 100,{A_{23}} = 0{/tex} and {tex}{A_{31}} = 75,{A_{32}} = 30,{A_{33}} = - 24{/tex} {tex}\therefore adj.A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {75}&{110}&{72} \\ {150}&{ - 100}&0 \\ {75}&{30}&{ - 24} \end{array}} \right]' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {75}&{150}&{75} \\ {110}&{ - 100}&{30} \\ {72}&0&{ - 24} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex}{A^{ - 1}} = \frac{{adj.A}}{{\left| A \right|}} = \frac{1}{{1200}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {75}&{150}&{75} \\ {110}&{ - 100}&{30} \\ {72}&0&{ - 24} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i), {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \frac{1}{{1200}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {75}&{150}&{75} \\ {110}&{ - 100}&{30} \\ {72}&0&{ - 24} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \frac{1}{{1200}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {300 + 150 + 150} \\ {440 - 100 + 60} \\ {288 + 0 - 48} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{1200}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {600} \\ {400} \\ {240} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{1}{2}} \\ {\frac{1}{3}} \\ {\frac{1}{5}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore u = \frac{1}{2},v = \frac{1}{3},w = \frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{1}{u} = 2,y = \frac{1}{v} = 3,z = \frac{1}{w} = 5{/tex}
solution: Put {tex}\frac{1}{x} = u,\frac{1}{y} = v{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{z} = w{/tex} in the given equations, {tex}2u + 3v + 10w = 4;{/tex} {tex}4u - 6v + 5w = 1;{/tex} {tex}6u + 9v - 20w = 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The matrix form of given equations is {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&{10} \\ 4&{ - 6}&5 \\ 6&9&{ - 20} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ v \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} [AX= B] Here, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&{10} \\ 4&{ - 6}&5 \\ 6&9&{ - 20} \end{array}} \right],X\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ v \\ z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left| A \right| = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&{10} \\ 4&{ - 6}&5 \\ 6&9&{ - 20} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= 2\left( {120 - 45} \right) - 3\left( { - 80 - 30} \right) + 10\left( {36 + 36} \right){/tex} {tex} = 150 + 330 + 750 = 1200 \ne 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore {A^{ - 1}}{/tex} exists and unique solution is {tex}X = {A^{ - 1}}B{/tex} ….(i) Now {tex}{A_{11}} = 75,{A_{12}} = 110,{A_{13}} = 72{/tex} and {tex}{A_{21}} = 150,{A_{22}} = - 100,{A_{23}} = 0{/tex} and {tex}{A_{31}} = 75,{A_{32}} = 30,{A_{33}} = - 24{/tex} {tex}\therefore adj.A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {75}&{110}&{72} \\ {150}&{ - 100}&0 \\ {75}&{30}&{ - 24} \end{array}} \right]' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {75}&{150}&{75} \\ {110}&{ - 100}&{30} \\ {72}&0&{ - 24} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex}{A^{ - 1}} = \frac{{adj.A}}{{\left| A \right|}} = \frac{1}{{1200}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {75}&{150}&{75} \\ {110}&{ - 100}&{30} \\ {72}&0&{ - 24} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i), {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \frac{1}{{1200}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {75}&{150}&{75} \\ {110}&{ - 100}&{30} \\ {72}&0&{ - 24} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \frac{1}{{1200}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {300 + 150 + 150} \\ {440 - 100 + 60} \\ {288 + 0 - 48} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{1200}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {600} \\ {400} \\ {240} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{1}{2}} \\ {\frac{1}{3}} \\ {\frac{1}{5}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore u = \frac{1}{2},v = \frac{1}{3},w = \frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{1}{u} = 2,y = \frac{1}{v} = 3,z = \frac{1}{w} = 5{/tex}
solution_html:
Put {tex}\frac{1}{x} = u,\frac{1}{y} = v{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{z} = w{/tex} in the given equations,

{tex}2u + 3v + 10w = 4;{/tex} {tex}4u - 6v + 5w = 1;{/tex} {tex}6u + 9v - 20w = 2{/tex} 

{tex}\therefore{/tex} The matrix form of given equations is {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&{10} \\ 4&{ - 6}&5 \\ 6&9&{ - 20} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ v \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}[AX= B]

Here, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&{10} \\ 4&{ - 6}&5 \\ 6&9&{ - 20} \end{array}} \right],X\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ v \\ z \end{array}} \right]{/tex}and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} 

{tex}\therefore \left| A \right| = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&{10} \\ 4&{ - 6}&5 \\ 6&9&{ - 20} \end{array}} \right|{/tex}

{tex}= 2\left( {120 - 45} \right) - 3\left( { - 80 - 30} \right) + 10\left( {36 + 36} \right){/tex}

{tex} = 150 + 330 + 750 = 1200 \ne 0{/tex}

{tex}\therefore {A^{ - 1}}{/tex}exists and unique solution is {tex}X = {A^{ - 1}}B{/tex}….(i)

Now {tex}{A_{11}} = 75,{A_{12}} = 110,{A_{13}} = 72{/tex} and {tex}{A_{21}} = 150,{A_{22}} = - 100,{A_{23}} = 0{/tex} and {tex}{A_{31}} = 75,{A_{32}} = 30,{A_{33}} = - 24{/tex}

{tex}\therefore adj.A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {75}&{110}&{72} \\ {150}&{ - 100}&0 \\ {75}&{30}&{ - 24} \end{array}} \right]' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {75}&{150}&{75} \\ {110}&{ - 100}&{30} \\ {72}&0&{ - 24} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

And {tex}{A^{ - 1}} = \frac{{adj.A}}{{\left| A \right|}} = \frac{1}{{1200}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {75}&{150}&{75} \\ {110}&{ - 100}&{30} \\ {72}&0&{ - 24} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} 

{tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i),

{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \frac{1}{{1200}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {75}&{150}&{75} \\ {110}&{ - 100}&{30} \\ {72}&0&{ - 24} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}} \right] = \frac{1}{{1200}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {300 + 150 + 150} \\ {440 - 100 + 60} \\ {288 + 0 - 48} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{1}{{1200}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {600} \\ {400} \\ {240} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{1}{2}} \\ {\frac{1}{3}} \\ {\frac{1}{5}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\therefore u = \frac{1}{2},v = \frac{1}{3},w = \frac{1}{5}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{1}{u} = 2,y = \frac{1}{v} = 3,z = \frac{1}{w} = 5{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Using matrix method, solve the system of equations
4x + 3y + 2z = 60;
x + 2y + 3z = 45;
6x + 2y + 3z = 70.
question_text: Using matrix method, solve the system of equations 4x + 3y + 2z = 60; x + 2y + 3z = 45; 6x + 2y + 3z = 70.
explanation: Given set of equations are:- 4x + 3y + 2z = 60 x + 2y + 3z = 45 6x + 2y + 3z = 70 Converting the following set of equations in matrix form, we have AX = B {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 6 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 60 \\ 45 \\ 70 \end{array}\right]{/tex} R 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 4R 2 - R 1 R 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2R 3 - 3R 1 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 0 & 5 & 10 \\ 0 & -5 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 60 \\ 120 \\ -40 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Again converting into equations, we get 4x + 3y + 2z = 60 5y + 10z = 120 -5y = - 40 Y = 8 5 × 8 + 10z = 120 10z = 120 - 40 10z = 80 z = 8 4x + 3 × 8 + 2 × 8 = 60 4x = 60 - 24 - 16 4x = 20 x = 5 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 5, y = 8, z = 8
solution: Given set of equations are:- 4x + 3y + 2z = 60 x + 2y + 3z = 45 6x + 2y + 3z = 70 Converting the following set of equations in matrix form, we have AX = B {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 6 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 60 \\ 45 \\ 70 \end{array}\right]{/tex} R 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 4R 2 - R 1 R 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2R 3 - 3R 1 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 0 & 5 & 10 \\ 0 & -5 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 60 \\ 120 \\ -40 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Again converting into equations, we get 4x + 3y + 2z = 60 5y + 10z = 120 -5y = - 40 Y = 8 5 × 8 + 10z = 120 10z = 120 - 40 10z = 80 z = 8 4x + 3 × 8 + 2 × 8 = 60 4x = 60 - 24 - 16 4x = 20 x = 5 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 5, y = 8, z = 8
solution_html:
Given set of equations are:-
4x + 3y + 2z = 60
x + 2y + 3z = 45
6x + 2y + 3z = 70
Converting the following set of equations in matrix form, we have
AX = B
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 6 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 60 \\ 45 \\ 70 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
R2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}4R2 - R1
R3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}2R3 - 3R1
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 0 & 5 & 10 \\ 0 & -5 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 60 \\ 120 \\ -40 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Again converting into equations, we get
4x + 3y + 2z = 60
5y + 10z = 120
-5y = - 40
Y = 8
5 × 8 + 10z = 120
10z = 120 - 40
10z = 80
z = 8
4x + 3 × 8 + 2 × 8 = 60
4x = 60 - 24 - 16
4x = 20
x = 5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 5, y = 8, z = 8

question_html:
Using matrix method, solve the system of equations
3x - 4y + 2z = -1;
2x + 3y + 5z = 7;
x + z = 2.
question_text: Using matrix method, solve the system of equations 3x - 4y + 2z = -1; 2x + 3y + 5z = 7; x + z = 2.
explanation: Given set of lines are:- 3x - 4y + 2z = -1 2x + 3y + 5z = 7; x + z = 2 Converting the following equations in matrix form, AX = B {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -4 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 5 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} -1 \\ 7 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 3R 2 - 2R 1 3R 3 - R 1 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -4 & 2 \\ 0 & 17 & 11 \\ 0 & 4 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} -1 \\ 23 \\ 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 11R 3 - R 2 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -4 & 2 \\ 0 & 17 & 11 \\ 0 & 27 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} -1 \\ 23 \\ 54 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Again converting into equations, we get 3x - 4y + 2z = -1 17y + 11z = 23 27y = 54 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 17 × 2 + 11z = 23 11z = 23 - 34 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = -1 3x - 4 × 2 + 2 × -1 = -1 3x = -1 + 8 + 2 3x = 9 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 3, y = 2, z = -1.
solution: Given set of lines are:- 3x - 4y + 2z = -1 2x + 3y + 5z = 7; x + z = 2 Converting the following equations in matrix form, AX = B {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -4 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 5 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} -1 \\ 7 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 3R 2 - 2R 1 3R 3 - R 1 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -4 & 2 \\ 0 & 17 & 11 \\ 0 & 4 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} -1 \\ 23 \\ 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 11R 3 - R 2 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -4 & 2 \\ 0 & 17 & 11 \\ 0 & 27 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} -1 \\ 23 \\ 54 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Again converting into equations, we get 3x - 4y + 2z = -1 17y + 11z = 23 27y = 54 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 17 × 2 + 11z = 23 11z = 23 - 34 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = -1 3x - 4 × 2 + 2 × -1 = -1 3x = -1 + 8 + 2 3x = 9 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 3, y = 2, z = -1.
solution_html:
Given set of lines are:-
3x - 4y + 2z = -1
2x + 3y + 5z = 7;
x + z = 2
Converting the following equations in matrix form,
AX = B
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -4 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 5 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} -1 \\ 7 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
3R2 - 2R1
3R3 - R1
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -4 & 2 \\ 0 & 17 & 11 \\ 0 & 4 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} -1 \\ 23 \\ 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
11R3 - R2
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -4 & 2 \\ 0 & 17 & 11 \\ 0 & 27 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} -1 \\ 23 \\ 54 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Again converting into equations, we get
3x - 4y + 2z = -1
17y + 11z = 23
27y = 54
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2
17 × 2 + 11z = 23
11z = 23 - 34
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = -1
3x - 4 × 2 + 2 × -1 = -1
3x = -1 + 8 + 2
3x = 9
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}x = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 3, y = 2, z = -1.

question_html:
If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & -2 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 2 & -6 \\ -2 & 1 & -3 \\ -4 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} find AB. Hence, solve the system of equations:
x - 2y = 10, 2x + y + 3z = 8 and - 2y + z = 7
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & -2 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 2 & -6 \\ -2 & 1 & -3 \\ -4 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} find AB. Hence, solve the system of equations: x - 2y = 10, 2x + y + 3z = 8 and - 2y + z = 7
explanation: Here: A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -2 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 2 & -6 \\ -2 & 1 & -3 \\ -4 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7+4-0 & 2-2+0 & -6+6+0 \\ 14-2-12 & 4+1+6 & -12-3+15 \\ 0-4+4 & 0-2+2 & 0+6+5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 11 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 11 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 11 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, we can see that it is AB = 11I. where I is the unit Matrix or, A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 2 & -6 \\ -2 & 1 & -3 \\ -4 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, the given equation can be written as: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -2 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 10 \\ 8 \\ 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} X = A –1 B {tex}=\frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 2 & -6 \\ -2 & 1 & -3 \\ -4 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 10 \\ 8 \\ 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{c} 70+16-42 \\ -20+8-21 \\ -40+16+35 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{c} 44 \\ -33 \\ 11 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}x=\left[\begin{array}{c} 4 \\ -3 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Hence, x = 4,y = – 3 and z = 1
solution: Here: A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -2 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 2 & -6 \\ -2 & 1 & -3 \\ -4 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7+4-0 & 2-2+0 & -6+6+0 \\ 14-2-12 & 4+1+6 & -12-3+15 \\ 0-4+4 & 0-2+2 & 0+6+5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 11 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 11 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 11 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, we can see that it is AB = 11I. where I is the unit Matrix or, A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 2 & -6 \\ -2 & 1 & -3 \\ -4 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, the given equation can be written as: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -2 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 10 \\ 8 \\ 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} X = A –1 B {tex}=\frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 2 & -6 \\ -2 & 1 & -3 \\ -4 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 10 \\ 8 \\ 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{c} 70+16-42 \\ -20+8-21 \\ -40+16+35 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{c} 44 \\ -33 \\ 11 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}x=\left[\begin{array}{c} 4 \\ -3 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Hence, x = 4,y = – 3 and z = 1
solution_html:
Here:
A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -2 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 2 & -6 \\ -2 & 1 & -3 \\ -4 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7+4-0 & 2-2+0 & -6+6+0 \\ 14-2-12 & 4+1+6 & -12-3+15 \\ 0-4+4 & 0-2+2 & 0+6+5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 11 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 11 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 11 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, we can see that it is AB = 11I. where I is the unit Matrix
or, A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 2 & -6 \\ -2 & 1 & -3 \\ -4 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, the given equation can be written as:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -2 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 10 \\ 8 \\ 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
X = A–1B
{tex}=\frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 2 & -6 \\ -2 & 1 & -3 \\ -4 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 10 \\ 8 \\ 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{c} 70+16-42 \\ -20+8-21 \\ -40+16+35 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{c} 44 \\ -33 \\ 11 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}x=\left[\begin{array}{c} 4 \\ -3 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Hence, x = 4,y = – 3 and z = 1

question_html: What is the value of the determinant {tex}\left| \begin{array} { l l l } { 0 } & { 2 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { 4 } & { 5 } & { 6 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}?
question_text: What is the value of the determinant {tex}\left| \begin{array} { l l l } { 0 } & { 2 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { 4 } & { 5 } & { 6 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} ?
explanation: We have to find the value of determinant {tex}\left| \begin{array} { l l l } { 0 } & { 2 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { 4 } & { 5 } & { 6 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Let {tex}\Delta = \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { 0 } & { 2 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { 4 } & { 5 } & { 6 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Let {tex}\Delta{/tex} = 2(12 - 16) [expanding along R 1 ] = -2(-4) = 8
solution: We have to find the value of determinant {tex}\left| \begin{array} { l l l } { 0 } & { 2 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { 4 } & { 5 } & { 6 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Let {tex}\Delta = \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { 0 } & { 2 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { 4 } & { 5 } & { 6 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Let {tex}\Delta{/tex} = 2(12 - 16) [expanding along R 1 ] = -2(-4) = 8

We have to find the value of determinant {tex}\left| \begin{array} { l l l } { 0 } & { 2 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { 4 } & { 5 } & { 6 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}

question_html:
What positive value of x makes following pair of determinants equal?
{tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x } & { 3 } \\ { 5 } & { x } \end{array} \right|{/tex}, {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 16 } & { 3 } \\ { 5 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
question_text: What positive value of x makes following pair of determinants equal? {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x } & { 3 } \\ { 5 } & { x } \end{array} \right|{/tex} , {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 16 } & { 3 } \\ { 5 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x } & { 3 } \\ { 5 } & { x } \end{array} \right| = \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 16 } & { 3 } \\ { 5 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} On expanding, we get 2x 2 - 15= 32 - 15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 2 - 15 = 17 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 2 - 32 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 16 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex} 4 Hence, for x = 4, given pair of determinants is equal.
solution: Let {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x } & { 3 } \\ { 5 } & { x } \end{array} \right| = \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 16 } & { 3 } \\ { 5 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} On expanding, we get 2x 2 - 15= 32 - 15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 2 - 15 = 17 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 2 - 32 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 16 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex} 4 Hence, for x = 4, given pair of determinants is equal.
solution_html:
Let {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x } & { 3 } \\ { 5 } & { x } \end{array} \right| = \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 16 } & { 3 } \\ { 5 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
On expanding, we get
2x2 - 15= 32 - 15
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x2 - 15 = 17
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x2 - 32 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 16
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex}4
Hence, for x = 4, given pair of determinants is equal.

question_text: Write the adjoint of the following matrix. {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { 4 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
explanation: We have to find the adjoint of the following matrix. {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { 4 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Let A = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { 4 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} , then adj (A)= {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { C _ { 11 } } & { C _ { 21 } } \\ { C _ { 12 } } & { C _ { 22 } } \end{array} \right]{/tex} , where C ij denotes the cofactor of a ij . Therefore adj (A)= {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 1 } \\ { - 4 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}

Therefore adj (A)= {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 1 } \\ { - 4 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}

We have to find the adjoint of the following matrix.
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { 4 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}

Let A ={tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { 4 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}, then adj (A)= {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { C _ { 11 } } & { C _ { 21 } } \\ { C _ { 12 } } & { C _ { 22 } } \end{array} \right]{/tex}, where
Cij denotes the cofactor of aij .

Therefore adj (A)={tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 1 } \\ { - 4 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}

question_html: If a, b, c are positive and unequal, show that the value  of  the determinant  {tex}\left| \begin{array} { l l l } { a } & { b } & { c } \\ { b } & { c } & { a } \\ { c } & { a } & { b } \end{array} \right|{/tex} is negative?
question_text: If a, b, c are positive and unequal, show that the value of the determinant {tex}\left| \begin{array} { l l l } { a } & { b } & { c } \\ { b } & { c } & { a } \\ { c } & { a } & { b } \end{array} \right|{/tex} is negative?
explanation: Let {tex} \Delta = \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { a } & { b } & { c } \\ { b } & { c } & { a } \\ { c } & { a } & { b } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Therefore, on applying R 1 {tex} \rightarrow{/tex} R 1 + R 2 + R 3 , we get, {tex} \Delta = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { a + b + c } & { a + b + c } & { a + b + c } \\ { b } & { c } & { a } \\ { c } & { a } & { b } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Therefore, on taking common (a + b + c) from R 1 , we get, {tex}\Delta = ( a + b + c ) \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { b } & { c } & { a } \\ { c } & { a } & { b } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Therefore, on applying C 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C 2 - C 1 and C 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C 3 - C 1 , we get, {tex}\Delta = ( a + b + c ) \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { b } & { c - b } & { a - b } \\ { c } & { a - c } & { b - c } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Therefore,on expanding along R 1 , we get, {tex}\Delta{/tex} = (a + b + c) [-(b - c) 2 - (a - b) (a - c)] = (a + b + c) [- (b 2 + c 2 - 2bc) - (a 2 - ac - ab + bc)] = (a+ b + c)[-b 2 - c 2 + 2bc - a 2 + ac +ab - bc] = ( a + b + c) (ab+ bc + ca - a 2 - b 2 - c 2 ) = - (a+ b + c)(a 2 + b 2 + c 2 - ab - bc - ca) = - {tex} \frac 12{/tex} (a + b + c) (2a 2 + 2b 2 + 2c 2 - 2bc - 2ca) = - {tex} \frac 12{/tex} (a+ b + c){(a - b) 2 + (b - c) 2 + (c -a) 2 } < 0 [ {tex} \because{/tex} a, b, c > 0 and a {tex} \ne{/tex} b {tex} \ne{/tex} c]
solution: Let {tex} \Delta = \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { a } & { b } & { c } \\ { b } & { c } & { a } \\ { c } & { a } & { b } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Therefore, on applying R 1 {tex} \rightarrow{/tex} R 1 + R 2 + R 3 , we get, {tex} \Delta = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { a + b + c } & { a + b + c } & { a + b + c } \\ { b } & { c } & { a } \\ { c } & { a } & { b } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Therefore, on taking common (a + b + c) from R 1 , we get, {tex}\Delta = ( a + b + c ) \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { b } & { c } & { a } \\ { c } & { a } & { b } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Therefore, on applying C 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C 2 - C 1 and C 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C 3 - C 1 , we get, {tex}\Delta = ( a + b + c ) \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { b } & { c - b } & { a - b } \\ { c } & { a - c } & { b - c } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Therefore,on expanding along R 1 , we get, {tex}\Delta{/tex} = (a + b + c) [-(b - c) 2 - (a - b) (a - c)] = (a + b + c) [- (b 2 + c 2 - 2bc) - (a 2 - ac - ab + bc)] = (a+ b + c)[-b 2 - c 2 + 2bc - a 2 + ac +ab - bc] = ( a + b + c) (ab+ bc + ca - a 2 - b 2 - c 2 ) = - (a+ b + c)(a 2 + b 2 + c 2 - ab - bc - ca) = - {tex} \frac 12{/tex} (a + b + c) (2a 2 + 2b 2 + 2c 2 - 2bc - 2ca) = - {tex} \frac 12{/tex} (a+ b + c){(a - b) 2 + (b - c) 2 + (c -a) 2 } < 0 [ {tex} \because{/tex} a, b, c > 0 and a {tex} \ne{/tex} b {tex} \ne{/tex} c]
solution_html:
Let {tex} \Delta = \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { a } & { b } & { c } \\ { b } & { c } & { a } \\ { c } & { a } & { b } \end{array} \right|{/tex}

Therefore, on applying R1 {tex} \rightarrow{/tex} R1 + R2 + R3, we get,
{tex} \Delta = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { a + b + c } & { a + b + c } & { a + b + c } \\ { b } & { c } & { a } \\ { c } & { a } & { b } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
Therefore, on taking common (a + b + c) from R1 , we get,
{tex}\Delta = ( a + b + c ) \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { b } & { c } & { a } \\ { c } & { a } & { b } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
Therefore, on applying C2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C2 - C1 and C3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C3 - C1, we get,
{tex}\Delta = ( a + b + c ) \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { b } & { c - b } & { a - b } \\ { c } & { a - c } & { b - c } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
Therefore,on expanding along R1 , we get,
{tex}\Delta{/tex} = (a + b + c) [-(b - c)2 - (a - b) (a - c)]
= (a + b + c) [- (b2 + c2 - 2bc) - (a2 - ac - ab + bc)]
= (a+ b + c)[-b2 - c2 + 2bc - a2 + ac +ab - bc]
= ( a + b + c) (ab+ bc + ca - a2 - b2 - c2)
= - (a+ b + c)(a2 + b2 + c2 - ab - bc - ca)
= -{tex} \frac 12{/tex}(a + b + c) (2a2 + 2b2 + 2c2 - 2bc - 2ca)
= -{tex} \frac 12{/tex}(a+ b + c){(a - b)2 + (b - c)2 + (c -a)2} < 0 [{tex} \because{/tex} a, b, c > 0 and a {tex} \ne{/tex} b {tex} \ne{/tex} c]

question_html: Using properties of determinant, prove the {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p x ^ { 3 } } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p y ^ { 3 } } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p z ^3 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}= (1 + pxyz) (x - y) (y - z) (z - x), where p is any scalar.
question_text: Using properties of determinant, prove the {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p x ^ { 3 } } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p y ^ { 3 } } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p z ^3 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} = (1 + pxyz) (x - y) (y - z) (z - x), where p is any scalar.
explanation: To prove {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p x ^ { 3 } } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p y ^ { 3 } } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p z ^3 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} = (1 + pxyz) (x - y) (y - z) (z - x) We shall make use of the properties of determinants to prove the required result. LHS = {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p x ^ { 3 } } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p y ^ { 3 } } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p z ^3 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| + \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { p x ^ { 3 } } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { p y^3 } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { p z^3 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| + p x y z \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} [taking common p from C 3 , x from R 1 , y from R 2 and z from R 3 in 2 nd determinant] Therefore,on interchanging C 1 and C 3 is 1 st determinant, we get LHS = {tex}- \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { x } \\ { 1 } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { y } \\ { 1 } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { z } \end{array} \right| + p x y z \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Therefore,on interchanging C 2 and C 3 in 1 st determinant, we get LHS = {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right| + p x y z \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex} =\quad ( 1 + p x y z ) \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Therefore,on applying R 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R 2 - R 1 and R 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R 3 -R 1 , we get, LHS = {tex}( 1 + p x y z ) \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 0 } & { y - x } & { ( y - x ) ( y + x ) } \\ { 0 } & { z - x } & { ( z - x ) ( z + x ) } \end{array} \right|{/tex} = (y - x)(z - x)(1 + pxyz) {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { y + x } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { z + x } \end{array} \right|{/tex} [taking common (y- x) from R 2 and (z- x) from R 3 ] = (y - x)(z - x)(1 + pxyz)(z + x - y - x) [expanding along C 1 ] = (1 + pxyz)(x - y)(y - z)(z - x) = RHS Hence proved.
solution: To prove {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p x ^ { 3 } } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p y ^ { 3 } } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p z ^3 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} = (1 + pxyz) (x - y) (y - z) (z - x) We shall make use of the properties of determinants to prove the required result. LHS = {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p x ^ { 3 } } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p y ^ { 3 } } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p z ^3 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| + \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { p x ^ { 3 } } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { p y^3 } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { p z^3 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| + p x y z \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} [taking common p from C 3 , x from R 1 , y from R 2 and z from R 3 in 2 nd determinant] Therefore,on interchanging C 1 and C 3 is 1 st determinant, we get LHS = {tex}- \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { x } \\ { 1 } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { y } \\ { 1 } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { z } \end{array} \right| + p x y z \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Therefore,on interchanging C 2 and C 3 in 1 st determinant, we get LHS = {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right| + p x y z \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex} =\quad ( 1 + p x y z ) \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} Therefore,on applying R 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R 2 - R 1 and R 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R 3 -R 1 , we get, LHS = {tex}( 1 + p x y z ) \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 0 } & { y - x } & { ( y - x ) ( y + x ) } \\ { 0 } & { z - x } & { ( z - x ) ( z + x ) } \end{array} \right|{/tex} = (y - x)(z - x)(1 + pxyz) {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { y + x } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { z + x } \end{array} \right|{/tex} [taking common (y- x) from R 2 and (z- x) from R 3 ] = (y - x)(z - x)(1 + pxyz)(z + x - y - x) [expanding along C 1 ] = (1 + pxyz)(x - y)(y - z)(z - x) = RHS Hence proved.
solution_html:
To prove {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p x ^ { 3 } } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p y ^ { 3 } } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p z ^3 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}= (1 + pxyz) (x - y) (y - z) (z - x)

We shall make use of the properties of determinants to prove the required result.

LHS = {tex} \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p x ^ { 3 } } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p y ^ { 3 } } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { 1 + p z ^3 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| + \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { p x ^ { 3 } } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { p y^3 } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { p z^3 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \\ { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \\ { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| + p x y z \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} [taking common p from C3, x from R1, y from R2 and z from R3 in 2nd determinant]
Therefore,on interchanging C1 and C3 is 1st determinant, we get
LHS = {tex}- \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x ^ { 2 } } & { x } \\ { 1 } & { y ^ { 2 } } & { y } \\ { 1 } & { z ^ { 2 } } & { z } \end{array} \right| + p x y z \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
Therefore,on interchanging C2 and C3 in 1st determinant, we get
LHS = {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right| + p x y z \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex} =\quad ( 1 + p x y z ) \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { y } & { y ^ { 2 } } \\ { 1 } & { z } & { z ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
Therefore,on applying R2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}R2 - R1 and R3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}R3 -R1, we get,
LHS = {tex}( 1 + p x y z ) \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 0 } & { y - x } & { ( y - x ) ( y + x ) } \\ { 0 } & { z - x } & { ( z - x ) ( z + x ) } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
= (y - x)(z - x)(1 + pxyz){tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { x } & { x ^ { 2 } } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { y + x } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { z + x } \end{array} \right|{/tex} [taking common (y- x) from R2 and (z- x) from R3]
= (y - x)(z - x)(1 + pxyz)(z + x - y - x) [expanding along C1]
= (1 + pxyz)(x - y)(y - z)(z - x) = RHS
Hence proved.

question_html:
Using matrix method, solve the system of equations
x + y + z = -1;
x - 2y + 3z = 2;
5x - 3y + z = 3.
question_text: Using matrix method, solve the system of equations x + y + z = -1; x - 2y + 3z = 2; 5x - 3y + z = 3.
explanation: Given set of lines are : - x + y + z = 1; x - 2y + 3z = 2; 5x - 3y + z = 3. Converting the following equations in matrix form, AX = B {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 & 3 \\ 5 & -3 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right] {/tex} R 2 - R 1 R 3 - 5R 1 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ 0 & -8 & -4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 1 \\ 1 \\ -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} R 3 + 2R 2 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ 0 & -14 & -0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Again converting into equations we get x + y + z = 1 -3y + 2z = 1 -14 y = 0 y = 0 -3 × 0 + 2z = 1 {tex}Z=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}x+0+\frac{1}{2}=1{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}: x=\frac{1}{2}, y=0, z=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} .
solution: Given set of lines are : - x + y + z = 1; x - 2y + 3z = 2; 5x - 3y + z = 3. Converting the following equations in matrix form, AX = B {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 & 3 \\ 5 & -3 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right] {/tex} R 2 - R 1 R 3 - 5R 1 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ 0 & -8 & -4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 1 \\ 1 \\ -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} R 3 + 2R 2 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ 0 & -14 & -0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Again converting into equations we get x + y + z = 1 -3y + 2z = 1 -14 y = 0 y = 0 -3 × 0 + 2z = 1 {tex}Z=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}x+0+\frac{1}{2}=1{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}: x=\frac{1}{2}, y=0, z=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Given set of lines are : -
x + y + z = 1;
x - 2y + 3z = 2;
5x - 3y + z = 3.
Converting the following equations in matrix form,
AX = B
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 & 3 \\ 5 & -3 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right] {/tex}
R2 - R1
R3 - 5R1
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ 0 & -8 & -4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 1 \\ 1 \\ -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
R3 + 2R2
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ 0 & -14 & -0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Again converting into equations we get
x + y + z = 1
-3y + 2z = 1
-14 y = 0
y = 0
-3 × 0 + 2z = 1
{tex}Z=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}x+0+\frac{1}{2}=1{/tex}
{tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}: x=\frac{1}{2}, y=0, z=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}.

question_html: Find the value of x from the following: {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} x & 4 \\ 2 & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0
question_text: Find the value of x from the following: {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} x & 4 \\ 2 & 2 x \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0
explanation: We have to find out the value of x from equation {tex}\left| \begin{array} { r r } { x } & { 4 } \\ { 2 } & { 2 x } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} . {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 2 - 8 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 2 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 4 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}x = \pm 2{/tex}
solution: We have to find out the value of x from equation {tex}\left| \begin{array} { r r } { x } & { 4 } \\ { 2 } & { 2 x } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} . {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 2 - 8 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 2 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 4 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}x = \pm 2{/tex}

We have to  find out the value of x from equation {tex}\left| \begin{array} { r r } { x } & { 4 } \\ { 2 } & { 2 x } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} .

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate :{tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c } { \sqrt { 6 } } & { \sqrt { 5 } } \\ { \sqrt { 20 } } & { \sqrt { 24 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} .
question_text: Evaluate : {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c } { \sqrt { 6 } } & { \sqrt { 5 } } \\ { \sqrt { 20 } } & { \sqrt { 24 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}\Delta = \left| \begin{array} { c c } { \sqrt { 6 } } & { \sqrt { 5 } } \\ { \sqrt { 20 } } & { \sqrt { 24 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 6 } \times \sqrt { 24 } - \sqrt { 5 } \times \sqrt { 20 }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 6 } \times \sqrt { 6 } \times \sqrt { 4 } - \sqrt { 5 } \times \sqrt { 5 } \times \sqrt { 4 }{/tex} {tex}= 6 \times 2 - 5 \times 2 = 12 - 10 = 2{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\Delta = \left| \begin{array} { c c } { \sqrt { 6 } } & { \sqrt { 5 } } \\ { \sqrt { 20 } } & { \sqrt { 24 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 6 } \times \sqrt { 24 } - \sqrt { 5 } \times \sqrt { 20 }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 6 } \times \sqrt { 6 } \times \sqrt { 4 } - \sqrt { 5 } \times \sqrt { 5 } \times \sqrt { 4 }{/tex} {tex}= 6 \times 2 - 5 \times 2 = 12 - 10 = 2{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\Delta = \left| \begin{array} { c c } { \sqrt { 6 } } & { \sqrt { 5 } } \\ { \sqrt { 20 } } & { \sqrt { 24 } } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 6 } \times \sqrt { 24 } - \sqrt { 5 } \times \sqrt { 20 }{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 6 } \times \sqrt { 6 } \times \sqrt { 4 } - \sqrt { 5 } \times \sqrt { 5 } \times \sqrt { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}= 6 \times 2 - 5 \times 2 = 12 - 10 = 2{/tex}

question_html: If A is a non-singular matrix of order 3 and | adj A| = |A| then what is the value of k?
question_text: If A is a non-singular matrix of order 3 and | adj A| = |A| then what is the value of k?
explanation: We know that, for a non-singular square matrix of order n |adj (A) | = |A| n- 1 Here, the order of A is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3, therefore n = 3 Hence, |adj (A) |= |A| 2 ...(i) But |adj (A)| = |A| [given]....(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get, k = 2
solution: We know that, for a non-singular square matrix of order n |adj (A) | = |A| n- 1 Here, the order of A is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3, therefore n = 3 Hence, |adj (A) |= |A| 2 ...(i) But |adj (A)| = |A| [given]....(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get, k = 2
solution_html:
We know that, for a non-singular square matrix of order n
|adj (A) | = |A| n- 1
Here, the order of A is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3, therefore n = 3
Hence, |adj (A) |= |A|2 ...(i)
But  |adj (A)| = |A|  [given]....(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get,
k = 2
